meta.generated_by: hand-written seed set + questions adapted from past exams (SS21-SS22, mock) + questions grounded in the lecture notes + questions grounded in the programming exercises 0-4

questions.question: What most distinguishes deep learning from classical machine learning?

options.A: It only works on image data
options.B: It learns feature representations directly from raw data instead of relying on hand-crafted features
options.D: It always needs less data to train

questions.explanation: Classical pipelines rely on hand-engineered features; deep networks learn the representation and the classifier jointly from raw data.

questions.question: Why are the networks in deep learning called 'deep'?

options.B: They stack many layers between the input and the output
options.D: They are trained on very large disks

questions.explanation: Depth refers to the number of processing layers between input and output, which is what allows hierarchical feature composition.

questions.question: Which event is widely credited with sparking the modern deep learning boom?

options.A: The first use of GPUs for gaming
options.B: The invention of the perceptron in 1958
options.D: AlexNet winning the ImageNet competition in 2012

questions.explanation: AlexNet's 2012 ImageNet win cut the error rate dramatically and convinced the vision community that deep CNNs were worth the compute.

questions.question: Which combination of factors best explains why deep learning became practical in the 2010s?

options.B: Large labelled datasets, GPU compute, and improved training methods
options.C: A switch away from neural networks

questions.explanation: None of the three on its own was enough: the ideas existed earlier, but large labelled datasets and GPU training made them practical.

questions.question: What does a single artificial neuron compute before applying its activation?

options.A: The product of all its inputs
options.D: A weighted sum of its inputs plus a bias

questions.explanation: The pre-activation is an affine function of the inputs; the activation function is then applied to that scalar.

questions.question: What is the purpose of forward propagation?

options.A: To update the weights using gradients
options.B: To pass inputs through the network and produce an output

questions.explanation: Forward propagation evaluates the network to obtain predictions; gradients are only computed later in the backward pass.

questions.question: What does backpropagation actually compute?

options.B: The gradients of the loss with respect to weights and biases

questions.explanation: Backpropagation is the chain rule applied layer by layer; the optimizer is a separate step that consumes those gradients.

questions.question: Why must a multilayer network use non-linear activations?

options.A: Otherwise the whole network collapses to a single linear transformation
options.B: Because linear functions cannot be differentiated
options.C: Otherwise the network cannot store weights
options.D: To make training slower on purpose

questions.explanation: A composition of affine maps is itself affine, so without non-linearities the depth adds no expressive power at all.

questions.question: In gradient descent, how are parameters updated?

options.A: By setting them to zero each step
options.C: In the direction of the gradient, scaled by the learning rate
options.D: In the direction opposite the gradient, scaled by the learning rate

questions.explanation: The gradient points uphill, so the update moves against it, scaled by the learning rate.

questions.question: Which of the following are symptoms or causes of the vanishing gradient problem? (Select all that apply)

options.A: Early layers learn very slowly or not at all
options.B: Gradients shrink toward zero in early layers of deep networks
options.C: It only occurs when the learning rate is set to zero
options.D: Saturating activations such as sigmoid or tanh make it worse

questions.explanation: Vanishing gradients arise from repeatedly multiplying small derivatives; the learning rate can worsen training but is not the cause.

questions.question: What does the Universal Approximation Theorem guarantee?

options.A: Any network trained long enough reaches zero test error
options.B: Gradient descent always finds the global optimum
options.C: Deeper networks always beat shallow ones
options.D: A network with one hidden layer and enough neurons can approximate any continuous function on a compact set

questions.explanation: It is an existence result about representability. It says nothing about how many units are needed or whether training will find them.

questions.question: How do residual (skip) connections help train very deep networks?

options.A: They give gradients a shortcut path, easing the vanishing-gradient and degradation problems
options.B: They remove the need for activation functions
options.C: They reduce the number of parameters to zero

questions.explanation: The identity path lets the gradient bypass a block, so the effective depth for gradient flow stays small even in very deep networks.

questions.question: Compared with widening a network, what does adding depth mainly provide?
questions.explanation: Each layer can build on the features of the previous one, so depth buys compositional structure that width alone does not.

questions.question: Which loss is standard for multi-class classification with a softmax output?
questions.explanation: Cross-entropy is the negative log-likelihood of the correct class under the softmax distribution, so the two pair naturally.

questions.question: Which loss is typically used for regression?
questions.explanation: Squared error corresponds to maximum likelihood under Gaussian noise, which is the usual assumption for continuous targets.

questions.question: How does stochastic gradient descent differ from full-batch gradient descent?

options.A: It updates using one (or a few) samples per step instead of the whole dataset
options.C: It uses the whole dataset per step

questions.explanation: SGD trades a noisy gradient estimate for far more updates per pass over the data, which usually converges much faster in wall-clock time.

questions.question: What does the learning rate control?

options.A: The size of each parameter update step

questions.explanation: Too large and the optimizer overshoots or diverges; too small and training crawls. It is usually the first hyperparameter to tune.

questions.question: What is the main benefit of using momentum in optimization?

options.A: It accumulates past gradients to smooth updates and speed up convergence
options.C: It increases the learning rate automatically to 1.0
options.D: It removes the need for a loss function

questions.explanation: Momentum accumulates a velocity across steps, damping oscillations across steep directions and accelerating along consistent ones.

questions.question: What two ideas does the Adam optimizer combine?

options.A: Momentum (first moment) and adaptive per-parameter scaling (second moment)

questions.explanation: Adam keeps a running first moment (momentum) and a running second moment, using the latter to scale each parameter's step individually.

questions.question: Why use a mini-batch rather than a single sample or the full dataset?

options.A: It is the only option that uses gradients
options.B: It balances gradient-estimate stability against computational efficiency
options.C: It avoids needing a learning rate
options.D: It removes all noise from the gradient

questions.explanation: Mini-batches average out much of the single-sample noise while still allowing many updates per epoch, and they map well onto GPU hardware.

questions.question: What problem does learning-rate scheduling (e.g. decay or cosine annealing) address?

options.A: It removes the need for a validation set
options.B: It forces the gradient to zero immediately
options.C: A fixed rate can be too large late in training, causing the loss to bounce around the minimum

questions.explanation: A rate that worked early on becomes too coarse near a minimum, so the loss bounces around instead of settling.

questions.question: Which statements about convergence and overfitting are correct? (Select all that apply)

options.A: Early stopping can be used to trade a little training fit for better validation performance
options.B: Convergence means the optimizer has settled near a minimum of the training loss
options.C: Overfitting means training loss keeps falling while validation loss rises
options.D: Reaching low training loss guarantees good generalization

questions.explanation: Low training loss says nothing about held-out performance, which is precisely why a separate validation set is needed.

questions.question: What is the output of the ReLU function for an input x?
questions.explanation: ReLU passes positive values unchanged and clamps everything else to zero, which is what makes it cheap and non-saturating.

questions.question: What is the output range of the sigmoid function?
questions.explanation: The sigmoid squashes the real line into the open interval (0, 1), which is why it can be read as a probability.

questions.question: What does a convolutional layer primarily do?

options.B: Normalizes the input to zero mean
options.C: Slides learnable filters over the input to extract local features
options.D: Flattens the image into a vector

questions.explanation: The same kernel is applied at every position, so the layer detects a local pattern wherever it occurs and shares its weights across the image.

questions.question: What is the purpose of a pooling layer?

options.A: To downsample feature maps, reducing spatial size and computation
options.C: To increase the number of channels

questions.explanation: Downsampling shrinks the spatial dimensions, cutting memory and computation while adding a little translation invariance.

questions.question: How does increasing the stride of a convolution affect the output feature map?

options.A: It doubles the number of channels
options.B: It has no effect on size

questions.explanation: A stride of s evaluates the kernel only every s positions, so each spatial dimension shrinks by roughly a factor of s.

questions.question: What does 'same' padding achieve with stride 1?

options.B: It keeps the output spatial size equal to the input
options.D: It converts the layer to fully connected

questions.explanation: Zero padding compensates for the pixels the kernel would otherwise lose at the border, keeping input and output the same size.

questions.question: Why is ReLU often preferred over sigmoid in hidden layers of deep CNNs?

options.A: It does not saturate for positive inputs, so gradients flow better

questions.explanation: The sigmoid's derivative is at most 0.25 and near zero in its tails, so stacking many sigmoid layers shrinks gradients rapidly.

questions.question: What is the key advantage of a dilated (atrous) convolution?

options.A: It reduces the number of channels
options.B: It is only used in fully connected layers
options.C: It enlarges the receptive field without increasing parameters or reducing resolution
options.D: It removes the need for activation functions

questions.explanation: Inserting gaps between kernel taps widens the receptive field exponentially with depth while the parameter count stays fixed.

questions.question: Given a 32x32 input, a 5x5 filter, stride 1 and no padding, what is the output width?
questions.explanation: With valid padding the output width is (W - k)/s + 1 = (32 - 5)/1 + 1 = 28.

questions.question: What is the goal of regularization?

options.A: To increase the number of parameters
options.B: To make training loss exactly zero
options.C: To improve generalization by discouraging overly complex models

questions.explanation: Regularization trades a little training accuracy for better generalization by restricting the effective complexity of the model.

questions.question: What does L2 regularization add to the loss?
questions.explanation: The squared-norm penalty is also called weight decay, because its gradient shrinks every weight proportionally on each update.

questions.question: What effect does L1 regularization tend to have on weights?

options.A: It has no effect on weights
options.C: It drives many weights exactly to zero (sparsity)

questions.explanation: The absolute-value penalty has a constant gradient magnitude, so it keeps pushing small weights until they hit exactly zero.

questions.question: How does dropout regularize a network?

options.A: By randomly deactivating neurons during training so the network cannot rely on any single one

questions.explanation: Because any unit may disappear, the network cannot rely on specific co-adapted partners and has to learn redundant features.

questions.question: What does batch normalization normalize?

options.B: The activations within a mini-batch to roughly zero mean and unit variance

questions.explanation: Each channel's activations are standardised using the statistics of the current mini-batch, then rescaled by learned parameters.

questions.question: Why can batch normalization allow higher learning rates?

options.C: It stabilises the distribution of layer inputs, reducing internal covariate shift
options.D: It sets the learning rate to 1.0

questions.explanation: Keeping layer inputs at a consistent scale stops a large step in one layer from throwing off every layer above it.

questions.question: What is early stopping?

options.A: Stopping after the first epoch always
options.B: Halting training once validation performance stops improving
options.C: Stopping when training loss reaches zero

questions.explanation: It uses the validation curve as the stopping signal, keeping the model from the point where generalization was best.

questions.question: Which of the following are legitimate regularization techniques? (Select all that apply)

options.B: Increasing the learning rate to infinity

questions.explanation: Dropout, weight decay and augmentation all restrict what the model can fit; an unbounded learning rate simply breaks training.

questions.question: What makes an RNN suited to sequential data?

options.A: It maintains a hidden state carried across time steps
options.C: It processes all inputs simultaneously with no order

questions.explanation: The hidden state acts as a memory summarising everything seen so far, so predictions can depend on arbitrary earlier inputs.

questions.question: Which gates does a standard LSTM cell use?
questions.explanation: The forget gate controls what leaves the cell state, the input gate what enters, and the output gate what is exposed as the hidden state.

questions.question: How does a GRU compare with an LSTM?

options.A: It has more gates and parameters
options.C: It uses fewer gates and parameters while often performing comparably
options.D: It is a type of CNN

questions.explanation: The GRU merges the forget and input gates and drops the separate cell state, giving a cheaper cell with broadly similar accuracy.

questions.question: Why do LSTMs handle long-term dependencies better than vanilla RNNs?

options.A: They use a cell state and gates that let information and gradients persist
options.B: They remove the hidden state entirely

questions.explanation: The cell state is updated additively rather than by repeated multiplication, so gradients can flow across many steps without shrinking.

questions.question: What does a bidirectional RNN add?

options.B: Processing the sequence in both forward and backward directions

questions.explanation: Running a second pass in reverse lets each position see future context too, which helps whenever the whole sequence is available up front.

questions.question: In a sequence-to-sequence model, what is the role of the encoder?

options.A: To generate the output tokens one by one
options.C: To compress the input sequence into a representation the decoder uses

questions.explanation: The encoder produces a representation of the source sequence that the decoder conditions on when generating the output.

questions.question: Why was the attention mechanism introduced on top of encoder-decoder RNNs?

options.A: To let the decoder focus on relevant encoder states instead of a single fixed vector
options.D: To avoid using any hidden state

questions.explanation: A single fixed-size context vector is a bottleneck for long inputs; attention lets the decoder read from all encoder states directly.

questions.question: What is teacher forcing during sequence model training?

options.A: Feeding the ground-truth previous token as input instead of the model's own prediction
options.C: Forcing all weights to be equal

questions.explanation: Feeding the true previous token stabilises early training, though it creates a mismatch with inference, where the model sees its own outputs.

questions.question: What defines unsupervised learning?

options.B: It finds structure in data without labels

questions.explanation: There is no target to compare against, so the objective is defined by the data itself, for example reconstruction or cluster compactness.

questions.question: What does k-means clustering do?

options.B: Partitions data into k groups by minimizing within-cluster distances

questions.explanation: It alternates between assigning points to the nearest centroid and recomputing centroids, converging to a local optimum.

questions.question: What is the main purpose of PCA?

options.A: To reduce dimensionality while preserving as much variance as possible
options.B: To increase the number of features

questions.explanation: PCA projects onto the leading eigenvectors of the covariance matrix, which are the directions of greatest variance in the data.

questions.question: What does an autoencoder learn?

options.A: A compressed latent representation by reconstructing its input

questions.explanation: The bottleneck forces the network to discard everything not needed to reconstruct the input, which is what makes the code informative.

questions.question: In a GAN, what are the two competing networks?
questions.explanation: The generator maps noise to samples and the discriminator tries to tell them from real data; training is the resulting min-max game.

questions.question: What advantage does DBSCAN have over k-means?

options.A: It requires the number of clusters in advance
options.B: It only works on labelled data
options.D: It can find arbitrarily shaped clusters and mark outliers as noise

questions.explanation: Density-based clustering needs no cluster count and, unlike k-means, is not restricted to roughly spherical, equally sized groups.

questions.question: Why are learned word embeddings useful?

options.B: They remove the need for any training
options.C: They store words as one-hot vectors
options.D: They place semantically similar words near each other in a dense vector space

questions.explanation: One-hot vectors are equidistant and carry no similarity; embeddings place related words close together in a dense, low-dimensional space.

questions.question: Why normalize or standardize input features?

options.A: To make features have wildly different scales
options.B: To put features on comparable scales, which helps optimization

questions.explanation: Features on very different scales make the loss surface elongated, so gradient descent zig-zags and needs a smaller learning rate.

questions.question: What is the purpose of a validation set?

options.A: To report the final published accuracy
options.C: To tune hyperparameters and monitor generalization during development

questions.explanation: It stands in for unseen data while you make modelling decisions; once you tune on it, it is no longer an unbiased estimate.

questions.question: What does k-fold cross-validation provide?

options.A: A replacement for the loss function
options.B: A more robust performance estimate by rotating which fold is held out
options.D: A way to increase the dataset size permanently

questions.explanation: Averaging over k splits reduces the variance of the estimate, which matters most when the dataset is small.

questions.question: What is transfer learning?

options.A: Reusing a model pre-trained on one task as a starting point for a related task
options.B: Training from random weights every time

questions.explanation: Features learned on a large source dataset often transfer, so the new task needs far less labelled data than training from scratch.

questions.question: Why prefer random search over grid search for many hyperparameters?

options.A: It never needs a validation set
options.B: It only works for two parameters
options.C: It explores important dimensions more efficiently when only a few matter
options.D: It is guaranteed to find the global optimum

questions.explanation: Grid search wastes trials repeating the same values of the parameters that do not matter, while random search samples each dimension afresh.

questions.question: What key idea does ResNet introduce?

options.A: Residual/skip connections enabling very deep networks

questions.explanation: Learning a residual relative to the identity makes very deep stacks trainable, since a block can default to passing its input through.

questions.question: What is VGG known for?
questions.explanation: Two stacked 3x3 convolutions have the receptive field of one 5x5 but fewer parameters and an extra non-linearity.

questions.question: What does an Inception module do?

options.D: Applies several filter sizes in parallel and concatenates the results

questions.explanation: Running several kernel sizes side by side lets the network choose the useful scale itself, with 1x1 convolutions keeping the cost down.

questions.question: What is the core mechanism of the Transformer architecture?

options.D: Self-attention relating all positions to each other

questions.explanation: Self-attention computes a weighted combination of all positions at once, so the path between any two positions has constant length.

questions.question: How does a Vision Transformer (ViT) feed an image into a Transformer?

options.A: By using only the first row of pixels
options.C: By converting it to audio first
options.D: By splitting the image into fixed-size patches treated as tokens

questions.explanation: Patches keep the sequence length manageable; one token per pixel would make the quadratic attention cost prohibitive.

questions.question: What does a saliency map show?

options.B: Which input regions most influence the model's output

questions.explanation: Saliency is computed from the gradient of the output with respect to the input, highlighting the pixels the prediction is most sensitive to.

questions.question: What is Grad-CAM used for?

options.C: Highlighting image regions that drive a CNN's prediction

questions.explanation: It weights the feature maps of a convolutional layer by their gradients, producing a coarse but class-specific heat map.

questions.question: In self-attention, what do the query, key and value come from?

options.C: Learned linear projections of the same input sequence

questions.explanation: All three are different learned projections of the same sequence, which is exactly what makes the attention 'self'.

questions.question: Why does a Transformer need positional encoding?

options.C: Because attention alone is order-agnostic and would ignore token positions
options.D: To reduce the number of parameters

questions.explanation: Attention is a set operation, so without an explicit position signal the model would treat a shuffled sequence identically.

questions.question: What does multi-head attention provide over single-head attention?

options.D: Several attention patterns in parallel, capturing different relationships

questions.explanation: Each head can specialise on a different relation, and their outputs are concatenated and projected back to the model dimension.

questions.question: What does Q-learning estimate?

options.A: The expected return of taking an action in a state

questions.explanation: The action-value function gives the expected return of an action in a state, so the greedy policy follows from taking its argmax.

questions.question: What is the exploration-exploitation trade-off?

options.C: Balancing width and depth of a network
options.D: Balancing trying new actions against using known good ones

questions.explanation: Exploiting maximises immediate reward but may miss a better option; exploring costs reward now in exchange for information.

questions.question: What does epsilon-greedy exploration do?

options.A: Picks a random action with probability epsilon, else the greedy action

questions.explanation: It is the simplest workable exploration scheme; epsilon is usually annealed so the agent explores early and exploits later.

questions.question: Why is experience replay used in DQN?

options.B: To reuse past transitions and break correlations between consecutive samples

questions.explanation: Consecutive transitions are highly correlated, which destabilises training; sampling a buffer restores something closer to i.i.d. batches.

questions.question: What role does the target network play in DQN?

options.D: It provides slowly-updated target values, stabilising training

questions.explanation: Bootstrapping from a network that is itself changing chases a moving target; a periodically copied network keeps the target fixed for a while.

questions.question: What does semantic segmentation produce?

options.C: A class label for every pixel
options.D: A single label for the whole image

questions.explanation: The output is a label map the same size as the input, with no distinction between separate objects of the same class.

questions.question: How does instance segmentation differ from semantic segmentation?

options.A: It separates individual object instances, not just classes
options.C: It produces one label for the image

questions.explanation: Two adjacent cars get one merged region under semantic segmentation but two separate masks under instance segmentation.

questions.question: What is the defining feature of the U-Net architecture?

options.A: It uses only fully connected layers
options.C: An encoder-decoder with skip connections between matching levels

questions.explanation: The skip connections carry high-resolution detail from the contracting path into the expanding path, sharpening the boundaries.

questions.question: What does a Fully Convolutional Network (FCN) replace to enable pixel-wise output?

options.B: The fully connected layers with convolutional layers

questions.explanation: Fully connected layers fix the input size and discard spatial layout; replacing them with convolutions preserves both.

questions.question: What does panoptic segmentation combine?
questions.explanation: Amorphous 'stuff' regions such as road or sky are handled semantically, while countable 'things' get individual instance masks.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a common technique to tackle class imbalance?

options.D: Weighting the loss with the inverse class frequency

questions.explanation: Batch normalization normalizes activations; it does nothing about imbalanced label distributions.

questions.question: What is ensembling?

options.A: Occluding different areas of the input image for visualization
options.B: Multiple networks trained on the same task whose predictions are combined, e.g. by majority vote
options.C: A network with multiple output heads for different tasks
options.D: Parallel paths with different kernel sizes inside one network

questions.explanation: An ensemble combines several independently trained models to reduce variance.

questions.question: You build a food-image classifier. Which of these dataset situations is NOT a confound?

options.A: Car images were shot in daylight while pedestrian images were shot in rain
options.B: Spanish audio was recorded with a different microphone than Italian audio
options.C: All T-shirt photos show women while all hoodie photos show men
options.D: Photos were taken with two different cameras, assigned randomly regardless of label

questions.explanation: A nuisance factor is only a confound when it correlates with the label; random camera assignment does not.

questions.question: You have a model pre-trained on a similar task. What is the standard way to reuse it for your new classification task?

options.A: Pick random layers to form a new model
options.B: Freeze the feature-extraction layers and retrain the classification layers at the end
options.C: Retrain the feature extractor and keep the original classifier

questions.explanation: Transfer learning typically keeps learned features and retrains the task-specific head.

questions.question: A dataset contains 80% dog images and 20% cat images. A model reaches 80% accuracy. Why is this not impressive?

options.A: A model that always predicts 'dog' already achieves 80%
options.C: The classes must first be normalized
options.D: Accuracy cannot be computed on imbalanced data

questions.explanation: With imbalance, the majority-class baseline sets the bar; 80% equals always predicting dog.

questions.question: A mask/no-mask classifier reaches 98.1% accuracy, but the test data contains 981 'mask' and 19 'no mask' samples. What is the main doubt?

options.A: The classes are highly imbalanced, so accuracy is misleading
options.B: The learning rate was too high
options.C: The confusion matrix must be symmetric

questions.explanation: With 98% majority class, near-98% accuracy can be achieved while failing the minority class; use balancing or loss weighting.

questions.question: Which is a suitable way to evaluate the FINAL performance of a fine-tuned network?

options.A: Mean precision on a mix of training, validation and test data
options.C: Receiver operating characteristics curve on the test set

questions.explanation: Final evaluation must use held-out test data; training-set metrics measure fit, not generalization.

questions.question: What is the name of the method that re-uses pre-trained weights for a related task?
questions.explanation: Reusing weights trained on one task as initialization for another is transfer learning.

questions.question: Which statement about input data normalization is true?

options.A: A common strategy is normalizing the data to zero mean and unit variance
options.B: It is unnecessary because networks adapt during training
options.C: It should be computed per sample independently
options.D: Test data should be normalized with statistics computed on the test set

questions.explanation: Standardization uses training-set statistics and is applied identically to validation/test data.

questions.question: Which of the following help to reduce overfitting? (Select all that apply)

options.B: Switching from SGD to full-batch gradient descent

questions.explanation: Augmentation and dropout regularize; batch mode and longer training do not reduce overfitting.

questions.question: Why does initialization matter for optimizing deep networks?

options.A: So training always reaches the same minimum
options.B: To reduce the number of parameters
options.D: Because the optimization problem is generally non-convex

questions.explanation: In a non-convex landscape, the starting point influences which minimum is reached and how fast.

questions.question: Which statement about weight/bias initialization is TRUE?

options.A: It is important to calibrate the variance of the weights
options.B: If the bias is 0, its gradient is always 0 too
options.C: Initializing the bias with 0 fixes the dying ReLU problem
options.D: Initialization matters because the problem is convex

questions.explanation: Schemes like Xavier/He scale the weight variance to the fan-in so activations neither vanish nor explode.

questions.question: Which statement about initialization is FALSE?

options.A: Initialization is important because deep learning problems are convex
options.B: Xavier initialization takes the number of input features into account
options.C: With all-zero weights, the gradient w.r.t. the weights is zero
options.D: A small positive bias can help against dying ReLUs

questions.explanation: The problems are non-convex; that is exactly why initialization matters.

questions.question: What is the correct order to train a network with backpropagation and SGD? (D: init weights, C: forward pass, A: compute error, E: update weights, B: iterate)
questions.explanation: Initialize, forward, measure error, update, and repeat until converged.

questions.question: All weights AND biases are initialized to zero. Hidden activations are ReLU, the final activation is a sigmoid. For input x=(1,1,1,1), the output is:
questions.explanation: Every pre-activation is 0, ReLU(0)=0, and the final sigmoid(0)=0.5.

questions.question: An unknown final activation produced the output -0.0001. Which function could it be?
questions.explanation: ReLU, sigmoid and softmax never output negative values; leaky ReLU (and tanh) can.

questions.question: Which function does NOT fulfill the requirements for a useful activation function in deep learning?
questions.explanation: 2x is linear, so stacking layers with it collapses to a single linear map.

questions.question: Why is the signum function not used as an activation in deep learning?

options.A: It is too expensive to compute
options.B: Its derivative is either 0 or undefined for all inputs
options.C: It does not introduce a non-linearity

questions.explanation: With zero/undefined derivative everywhere, no gradient can flow, so gradient-based training fails.

questions.question: Which function models the all-or-nothing response of biological neurons?
questions.explanation: A threshold unit fires fully once the input exceeds a level and stays silent otherwise.

questions.question: Can the XOR problem be solved by a single-layer perceptron?

options.A: No — at least one hidden layer is needed
options.D: No — at least two hidden layers are needed

questions.explanation: XOR is not linearly separable; one hidden layer with a non-linearity suffices.

questions.question: Two fully connected layers with NO activation in between (ŷ = σ(W₂(W₁x + b₁) + b₂)) are equivalent to a single layer ŷ = σ(W̃x + b̃) with:
questions.explanation: Multiplying out gives W₂W₁x + W₂b₁ + b₂ — without non-linearities depth adds nothing.

questions.question: Your model gives very different predictions across training runs, yet the average performance is high. The model has:
questions.explanation: Run-to-run spread is variance; good average performance indicates low bias.

questions.question: What is the purpose of the backpropagation algorithm?

options.C: It computes all gradients required for optimizing the network

questions.explanation: Backprop applies the chain rule to obtain gradients; the optimizer then performs the update.

questions.question: A friend proposes the activation f(x)=0 for x<-0.5, x on [-0.5,0.5], 1 for x>0.5 and claims SGD trains well with it. What is the problem?

options.A: It cannot be evaluated on GPUs
options.D: The gradient vanishes almost everywhere outside [-0.5, 0.5]

questions.explanation: Outside the linear part the derivative is exactly zero, so saturated units get no learning signal.

questions.question: A network block computes ŷ = σ₂(x + w₂·σ₁(b₁ + w₁x)). Which architecture does this resemble?
questions.explanation: The '+ x' is a skip (identity) connection around the transformation — the ResNet idea.

questions.question: For a binary classifier whose single output should lie between 0 and 1, which final activation is appropriate?
questions.explanation: A scalar probability needs the sigmoid; softmax on one scalar always outputs 1.

questions.question: Which loss matches binary classification with a sigmoid output f(x) and label y ∈ {0,1}?
questions.explanation: This is binary cross-entropy, the maximum-likelihood loss under a Bernoulli model.

questions.question: A confusion matrix shows 980 true positives, 18 false positives, 1 false negative, 1 true negative (1000 samples). The accuracy is:
questions.explanation: (980+1)/1000 = 98.1% — and it hides the near-total failure on the negative class.

questions.question: Bad design or fine? A binary classifier ends with ReLU, then a sigmoid, and everything with sigmoid ≥ 0.5 is 'positive'.

options.A: Bad — ReLU output is ≥ 0, so the sigmoid is always ≥ 0.5 and everything is classified positive
options.C: Fine — the threshold adapts during training
options.D: Bad — sigmoid cannot follow ReLU technically

questions.explanation: sigmoid(z) ≥ 0.5 for all z ≥ 0; after ReLU there are no negative inputs left.

questions.question: Training loss curves for the same task: which noise ordering is expected?

options.B: SGD noisiest, mini-batch in between, full-batch smoothest

questions.explanation: Smaller batches give noisier gradient estimates; the full batch gives the smoothest curve.

questions.question: You train with FULL-batch gradient descent and the data is not shuffled. Does shuffling help?

options.B: No, shuffling is never useful in deep learning
options.D: No — each update uses the whole training set, so the order within it is irrelevant

questions.explanation: Order matters for stochastic/mini-batch updates, not when the full set is averaged per step.

questions.question: A conditional GAN is trained with mini-batches where ALL dog images come first, then all cat images. A colleague says you must shuffle. Right?

options.A: No, shuffling only matters for full-batch training
options.C: Yes — with sorted data the loss (and the generator's output) shifts drastically between class blocks, making optimization much harder
options.D: Yes, but only because of batch normalization

questions.explanation: Mini-batch updates follow the current batch distribution; class-sorted streams cause drift and instability.

questions.question: What is the purpose of momentum in optimizers?

options.A: It averages samples inside a batch
options.C: It stabilizes training by keeping a moving average over previous gradients
options.D: It manipulates the learning rate to avoid exploding gradients

questions.explanation: Momentum accumulates past gradient directions, accelerating consistent directions and damping oscillation.

questions.question: What distinguishes Nesterov Accelerated Gradient (NAG) from classical momentum?

options.A: It evaluates the gradient at a look-ahead position (after the momentum step) instead of the current weights
options.C: It only works with full-batch training
options.D: It removes the momentum term entirely

questions.explanation: NAG 'peeks ahead' along the velocity before computing the gradient, giving a correction effect.

questions.question: For an already-trained network f and an image x with label y=0, an adversarial example is created by optimizing:

options.A: The input x itself, e.g. minimizing L = ||f(x) - y||² = ||f(x)||² w.r.t. x
options.C: The weights of f to minimize ||f(x)||²

questions.explanation: Adversarial attacks hold the weights fixed and take gradient steps on the input.

questions.question: Minimizing binary cross-entropy corresponds to maximum-likelihood estimation under which distribution?
questions.explanation: Two mutually exclusive outcomes with probability p and 1-p follow a Bernoulli distribution.

questions.question: Which final activation should be used for a multi-class problem with one-hot-encoded, mutually exclusive labels?
questions.explanation: Softmax produces a probability distribution over the classes that sums to 1.

questions.question: The same model must predict 'contains horse' AND 'contains dog' (both can be true). Which output activation is suitable?

options.B: One sigmoid per output, since the labels are not mutually exclusive

questions.explanation: Multi-label problems need independent per-class probabilities; softmax would force competition.

questions.question: Predictions with threshold 0.5 — image a: p(Horse)=0.8 (true: yes), b: p=0.4 (true: yes), c: p=0.7 (true: yes). The accuracy for class 'Horse' is:
questions.explanation: Predictions yes/no/yes vs truth yes/yes/yes → two of three correct.

questions.question: Which statement about the softmax as final layer is FALSE?

options.A: It assigns a probability to each class
options.B: It produces one vector per sample for multi-class problems
options.C: The outputs for one sample depend on each other
options.D: It cannot be used during testing

questions.explanation: Softmax is used at test time as well; only the loss computation is dropped.

questions.question: Which statement about implementing a convolutional layer is FALSE?

options.A: A 'valid' convolution outputs an image of the same size as its input
options.B: A stride larger than one reduces the spatial size
options.C: The number of kernels determines the number of output channels
options.D: The number of kernel weights is independent of the image size

questions.explanation: 'Valid' mode shrinks the output by kernel_size-1; 'same' padding keeps the size.

questions.question: Which statements about a convolutional layer are TRUE? (Select all that apply)

options.A: It can process inputs of arbitrary spatial size
options.B: The total number of weights depends on the stride
options.C: The total number of weights depends on the number of input channels

questions.explanation: Kernel weights scale with input channels; stride only affects the output size. Convolutions are translation-equivariant, not rotation-invariant.

questions.question: Which properties do convolutional networks exploit compared to fully connected ones? (Select all that apply)
questions.explanation: Convolutions reuse a small local kernel across positions, matching image structure.

questions.question: For a 2-D convolution with odd kernel width k and stride 1, 'same' output size requires padding per side of:
questions.explanation: Half the kernel overhang on each side: n_p = (k-1)/2 for odd k.

questions.question: A stride-s convolution is implemented as a stride-1 convolution followed by subsampling. What must the backward pass do?

options.B: Pad the incoming error tensor with zeros at the rows/columns that were discarded in the forward pass
options.D: Average the error tensor over the stride window

questions.explanation: Discarded positions received no output, so their gradient contribution is zero — insert zeros there.

questions.question: In max pooling, how is the gradient routed in the backward pass?

options.C: Evenly to all positions of the window
options.D: Entirely to the position that held the maximum in the forward pass

questions.explanation: Only the argmax influenced the output, so it receives the full gradient; the rest get zero.

questions.question: A (10,10,3) image: option 1 flattens it and uses a fully connected layer with 5 outputs; option 2 uses a conv layer with five 10×10 kernels in 'valid' mode. Which is true?
questions.explanation: A full-size valid kernel per output neuron is exactly a fully connected layer in disguise.

questions.question: What is the purpose of a 1×1 convolution ('bottleneck layer')?

options.B: It computes inner products per position and can reduce the channel dimension, saving parameters and computation

questions.explanation: 1×1 convolutions mix channels pointwise — a cheap, learnable dimensionality reduction.

questions.question: A CONV-3-32 layer ('same' padding, stride 1) processes a 32×32×3 input. How many weights does it have (ignoring biases)?
questions.explanation: Each of the 32 kernels spans 3 input channels with a 3×3 window: 32·3·9.

questions.question: What are feature maps in a CNN?

options.C: The activations generated by applying the kernels

questions.explanation: Each kernel produces one activation map showing where its pattern responds.

questions.question: What is the difference between convolution and cross-correlation?

options.C: Convolution flips the kernel; cross-correlation does not

questions.explanation: A convolution equals a cross-correlation with a flipped kernel and vice versa — deep learning frameworks typically implement cross-correlation.

questions.question: Which statements about batch normalization are TRUE? (Select all that apply)

options.A: It normalizes the weights of each layer
options.B: It can be skipped during inference
options.C: It normalizes the input distribution of the following layer

questions.explanation: BN standardizes activations and rescales them with learnable γ and β; at test time it runs with stored statistics.

questions.question: Which effect is credited to batch normalization during training?

options.C: It eliminates the need for a learning rate

questions.explanation: A smoother optimization landscape allows higher learning rates and faster convergence.

questions.question: How does batch normalization behave differently between training and testing?

options.A: Testing recomputes statistics on the test set
options.C: Training uses batch statistics and updates a moving average; testing uses the stored (training) statistics

questions.explanation: Per-batch statistics would make test predictions depend on the batch — hence frozen moving averages.

questions.question: Comparing weight histograms after training: run 1 shows a sharp peak at exactly 0, run 2 shows a narrower Gaussian than the unregularized run. Which norms were used?
questions.explanation: L1 drives weights exactly to zero (sparsity); L2 shrinks all weights, reducing the variance.

questions.question: How does the keep probability in dropout relate to the strength of regularization?

options.A: The keep probability has no influence
options.B: Lower keep probability (dropping more units) means stronger regularization
options.C: Higher keep probability means stronger regularization

questions.explanation: Dropping more units forces more redundancy — keep probability 1.0 disables dropout entirely.

questions.question: Histograms of activations drift away from mean 0 / variance 1 layer by layer although inputs were normalized. Name the problem and a counter-measure.

options.C: Internal covariate shift — add batch normalization layers

questions.explanation: The shifting layer-input distribution is countered by normalizing activations inside the network.

questions.question: What is the general purpose of regularization?

options.A: To reach zero training loss faster
options.D: To trade a bit of training fit for better generalization on unseen data

questions.explanation: Regularization counters overfitting — the bias/variance trade-off in action.

questions.question: Which statements about adversarial examples are TRUE? (Select all that apply)

options.B: They cannot occur in real photographs
options.C: They also exist for other ML methods such as SVMs
options.D: A slightly perturbed input can yield a completely different prediction

questions.explanation: Adversarial fragility is a general phenomenon of learned decision boundaries, not a GAN artifact.

questions.question: Which sampling strategy for generating sequences from a trained RNN does NOT exist?
questions.explanation: Greedy, beam and random sampling are standard; 'recognition sampling' is made up.

questions.question: What role does the hidden state play in an Elman RNN?

options.A: It is only used during testing
options.D: It carries time-dependent context — a short-term memory connecting time steps

questions.explanation: The hidden state is what makes the network recurrent: information flows from step to step.

questions.question: What advantage does an LSTM have over a simple RNN?

options.D: A cell state that serves as long-term memory, making long dependencies easier to learn

questions.explanation: The gated cell state provides an almost unimpeded gradient path across many time steps.

questions.question: During TBPTT, why is it incorrect to update the hidden-gate weights WHILE iterating backwards through the time series?

options.A: The learning rate would need rescaling
options.C: Weights may only be updated once per epoch
options.D: The cell shares its weights across all time steps — changing them mid-backward changes the function being differentiated, so the gradients no longer match

questions.explanation: The remedy is to accumulate the gradients over time and apply one update after the backward pass.

questions.question: Truncated backpropagation through time (TBPTT) addresses which problem, and how?

options.C: The high cost of a full-sequence update — by splitting long sequences into smaller, overlapping chunks
options.D: Overfitting — by dropping time steps randomly

questions.explanation: Updating only after a full long sequence is expensive; TBPTT updates on overlapping sub-sequences.

questions.question: Which problems are associated with the simple Elman cell? (Select all that apply)

options.B: Small weights multiplied over many steps can cause vanishing gradients

questions.explanation: Repeated multiplication with the same weights shrinks (or explodes) gradients, and old context fades.

questions.question: In a recurrent cell without a bounded non-linearity, all inputs xₜ > 0 and weights w_hh, w_xh > 0. For long sequences one risks:

options.A: Exploding gradients — a tanh in a standard Elman cell would bound the state to (-1,1)

questions.explanation: Positive weights repeatedly multiplied grow without bound; the tanh in standard cells limits the state.

questions.question: A binary-cross-entropy loss is attached directly to a recurrent cell whose output is not bounded. What problem occurs?

options.C: The logarithm cannot handle values outside [0,1] — a sigmoid must squash the outputs first

questions.explanation: ln(ŷ) requires ŷ in (0,1); without a sigmoid the loss is undefined for out-of-range outputs.

questions.question: Which application is a natural fit for a recurrent neural network?

options.C: Analyzing ECG signals, where neighboring samples are correlated in time

questions.explanation: RNNs shine when successive samples carry temporal dependencies (ECG, weather, language).

questions.question: In an encoder-decoder network, which part can be used on its own to create synthetic samples?
questions.explanation: Sampling the latent distribution and decoding generates new data; the VAE decoder is the generator.

questions.question: In an autoencoder figure, the part that maps the input to the bottleneck and the part that reconstructs from it are called:
questions.explanation: Encoder compresses, decoder reconstructs — the bottleneck in between is the code.

questions.question: What is the main difference between U-Nets and plain autoencoders?

options.D: U-Nets add skip connections from encoder to decoder levels

questions.explanation: The skips carry high-resolution detail past the bottleneck — crucial for segmentation.

questions.question: How is a DENOISING autoencoder trained?

options.A: On labels from a human annotator
options.D: The input is corrupted with a noise model (e.g. Gaussian) and the network learns to reconstruct the original

questions.explanation: Reconstructing clean data from corrupted input forces robust representations — and enables in-painting.

questions.question: What distinguishes a VARIATIONAL autoencoder from a standard one?

options.C: It compresses inputs into a constrained latent DISTRIBUTION (e.g. Gaussian) instead of a free latent vector

questions.explanation: The latent space is regularized toward a known distribution, so sampling from it yields valid new data.

questions.question: A linear autoencoder (no non-linearities) trained with L2 loss learns a generalization of:
questions.explanation: Without non-linearities the optimal bottleneck spans the principal subspace of the data.

questions.question: How is a GAN trained?

options.A: A min-max game: the discriminator maximizes classification of real vs. fake, the generator minimizes the discriminator's success
options.B: Generator and discriminator minimize the same loss together

questions.explanation: Generator maps noise to samples; discriminator judges real vs. generated — adversarial objectives.

questions.question: You want users to pick 'forest', 'lake', 'mountains' etc. and get matching generated images. Which GAN type and training data do you need?

options.A: A conditional GAN — both networks receive a class encoding, and the training images need environment labels
options.B: A GAN trained on noise alone
options.D: A vanilla GAN with unlabeled images

questions.explanation: Conditioning inputs steer generation; training therefore needs (image, label) pairs.

questions.question: After 100 GAN epochs the loss values look like at the start. Must you assume nothing was learned?

options.A: Yes, constant loss always means no progress
options.B: Yes, GAN losses must go to zero
options.C: No — generator and discriminator may both have improved equally well, keeping the losses balanced
options.D: No, losses are meaningless in general

questions.explanation: In the adversarial game, mutual improvement can leave the equilibrium losses unchanged.

questions.question: Test users complain that all images from your GAN look nearly identical. What happened, and what is a counter-strategy?

options.A: Mode collapse — mini-batch discrimination or unrolled GANs

questions.explanation: The generator found one output family that fools the discriminator; diversity penalties counteract it.

questions.question: Which architectures contain BOTH convolutional layers and skip connections? (Select all that apply)
questions.explanation: U-Net skips encoder features to the decoder; ResNet adds identity shortcuts. LeNet/VGG have neither.

questions.question: Which features are NOT used in LeNet? (Select all that apply)
questions.explanation: LeNet used convolutions, subsampling and tanh/sigmoid — ReLU and skips came later.

questions.question: What are advantages of making a network deeper? (Select all that apply)

options.C: Ability to approximate increasingly complex functions

questions.explanation: Depth builds feature hierarchies and expressiveness — at the cost of memory, not in its favor.

questions.question: Roughly how does model capacity relate to a network's design?

options.A: It is linked to the variety of functions the network can approximate and grows with depth/parameters
options.B: It is fixed by the number of training samples
options.D: It only depends on the optimizer

questions.explanation: Capacity describes the richness of representable functions — closely tied to the bias/variance trade-off.

questions.question: A pretrained CNN ends with flatten + fully connected layers and was trained on 32×32 images. To process arbitrarily-sized images while keeping a length-10 output, you should:

options.C: Replace flatten + FC with 1×1 (or n×n) convolutions and global average pooling

questions.explanation: Convolutional heads are size-agnostic; global pooling collapses any spatial extent to a fixed vector.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a technique to visualize important image areas of a classifier?
questions.explanation: Inception is an architecture module, not a visualization method.

questions.question: How are saliency maps generated, and what serves as the pseudo loss?

options.D: The classification score of the target class is used as pseudo loss and its gradient w.r.t. the input is visualized

questions.explanation: Backpropagating the class score to the input shows which pixels influence the decision most.

questions.question: Your saliency maps show the network ignores the face for smile detection. Training it additionally on related tasks (glasses detection, pose) is called:
questions.explanation: Auxiliary tasks share features and can steer attention toward the relevant regions.

questions.question: In reinforcement learning, what is the difference between exploitation and exploration?

options.A: Exploitation uses the currently known best action; exploration tries potentially suboptimal new actions to find better returns
options.D: Exploration is only used during testing

questions.explanation: Balancing both is essential — pure exploitation gets stuck in locally optimal behavior.

questions.question: Which statement about reinforcement learning is CORRECT?

options.A: Exploration should be used during testing
options.B: Exploration should be used during training to try out new action sequences
options.C: Exploiting one known good action suffices during training

questions.explanation: Training needs exploration (e.g. epsilon-greedy); at test time the learned policy is exploited.

questions.question: Which statement about RL methods is WRONG?

options.A: A good policy aims to maximize the future return
options.B: Q-learning is an off-policy method that needs no model of the environment dynamics
options.C: Temporal-difference learning is an on-policy method that needs information about the environment dynamics
options.D: The Bellman equations form a linear system solvable for small problems

questions.explanation: TD learning is model-free — it learns from sampled transitions without knowing the dynamics.

questions.question: In epsilon-greedy action selection, the agent:

options.D: Takes a random action with probability ε and the currently best-valued action otherwise

questions.explanation: ε controls the exploration rate; the rest of the time the highest Q-value action is exploited.

questions.question: Why does a purely greedy policy on immediate rewards fail in grid-world style tasks?

options.A: It only exploits the next action's reward and cannot look ahead over multiple steps

questions.explanation: Maximizing one-step reward ignores state transitions and long-term return.

questions.question: In the RL interaction loop, the agent sends ___ to the environment and receives ___ back.

options.B: actions; the next state and a reward

questions.explanation: Agent acts (aₜ); environment answers with state sₜ₊₁ and reward rₜ₊₁.

questions.question: What are the two main tasks of object detection?

options.D: Finding bounding boxes and classifying them

questions.explanation: Detection = where (localization via boxes) + what (class per box).

questions.question: Which statement about YOLO vs. (Fast) R-CNN is TRUE?

options.A: YOLO produces candidate regions that are passed to a CNN
options.C: Fast R-CNN does real-time detection while YOLO cannot
options.D: YOLO combines bounding-box prediction and classification in ONE network

questions.explanation: That single forward pass is why YOLO is called a single-shot detector and runs in real time.

questions.question: Why is YOLO called a 'Single-Shot Detector' (SSD)?

options.B: It needs a single training image
options.D: It combines box prediction and classification in one model and one forward pass

questions.explanation: No separate proposal stage — everything happens in one pass.

questions.question: Which statements are TRUE? (Select all that apply)

options.A: Instance segmentation can only find one instance per class
options.B: Semantic segmentation can be considered pixel-wise classification
options.C: The goal of segmentation is drawing bounding boxes
options.D: A pixel-wise segmentation of an object can be converted into a bounding box

questions.explanation: Segmentation labels pixels; boxes follow from the mask extent. Instance segmentation separates many instances.

questions.question: You must check whether the number of people in a room is below capacity. Semantic segmentation or object detection?

options.A: Object detection, because detecting person instances allows counting them
options.C: Semantic segmentation, because it labels every pixel

questions.explanation: Counting needs instances; semantic masks of 'person' pixels merge people together.

questions.question: In segmentation evaluation with pᵢⱼ = pixels of true class i predicted as class j, the metric Σᵢ pᵢᵢ / Σᵢ Σⱼ pᵢⱼ is:
questions.explanation: Correct pixels over all pixels — the plain pixel accuracy.

questions.question: With the same notation, the per-class Intersection over Union of class i is:
questions.explanation: Intersection pᵢᵢ over union = predicted ∪ ground-truth pixels of the class (counting the overlap once).

questions.question: Which metrics are commonly used to evaluate semantic segmentation? (Select all that apply)
questions.explanation: Perplexity is a language-model metric; the others are standard for segmentation.

questions.question: Why are fully connected layers on flattened pixels ill-suited for segmenting street scenes?

options.A: They are not translation invariant and need vastly more parameters than convolutions
options.B: They cannot be trained with SGD
options.C: They cannot output more than one value

questions.explanation: Reoccurring objects at different positions need shared, local filters — exactly what convolutions provide.

questions.question: Who introduced the perceptron, the ancestor of modern neural networks?
questions.explanation: Frank Rosenblatt proposed the perceptron in 1958.

questions.question: The demonstration that single-layer perceptrons cannot solve XOR contributed to:

options.A: An 'AI winter' with reduced neural-network research

questions.explanation: Minsky and Papert's analysis dampened enthusiasm for perceptrons for years.

questions.question: In the classical pattern-recognition pipeline, what does deep learning replace?

options.A: Hand-crafted feature extraction — features are learned from data

questions.explanation: End-to-end learning folds feature design into the trainable model.

questions.question: Which triple best explains deep learning's breakthrough around 2012?

options.A: Large labeled datasets, GPU compute, improved training techniques
options.B: Small data, slow CPUs, shallow nets

questions.explanation: ImageNet-scale data plus GPUs plus tricks like ReLU/dropout enabled AlexNet's win.

questions.question: Supervised learning differs from unsupervised learning in that it:

options.C: Learns from input-label pairs rather than from unlabeled data

questions.explanation: Supervision means ground-truth targets guide the loss.

questions.question: Why are separate training and test sets essential?

options.C: To measure generalization on data the model has never seen

questions.explanation: Evaluating on training data measures memorization, not generalization.

questions.question: Which of these is a typical deep learning application area?

options.A: Computer vision, speech recognition and NLP

questions.explanation: Perception-style tasks with raw high-dimensional inputs are the classic strongholds.

questions.question: The Rosenblatt perceptron classifies by:

options.B: Taking the sign of a weighted sum of the inputs plus bias

questions.explanation: sign(wᵀx + b) yields the binary decision.

questions.question: Geometrically, a single perceptron separates the input space with:
questions.explanation: The decision boundary wᵀx + b = 0 is linear.

questions.question: What does one full pass over the entire training set during training define?
questions.explanation: Epoch = every training sample seen once; iterations count individual updates.

questions.question: A learning rate chosen far too LARGE typically causes:

options.A: Divergence or oscillation around the minimum

questions.explanation: Steps overshoot the minimum; the loss can bounce or explode.

questions.question: A learning rate chosen far too SMALL typically causes:

options.C: Extremely slow convergence and risk of getting stuck early

questions.explanation: Tiny steps crawl through the landscape and may stall in flat regions.

questions.question: Backpropagation is essentially an efficient application of:

options.A: The chain rule of calculus on the computational graph

questions.explanation: Gradients are propagated backwards node by node, reusing intermediate results.

questions.question: In high-dimensional loss landscapes, the more common obstacle (compared to bad local minima) is:
questions.explanation: In many dimensions most critical points are saddles; flat regions slow training.

questions.question: Which statement about the bias term in a neuron is correct?

options.B: It shifts the activation threshold, like the intercept b in y = ax + b

questions.explanation: Without a bias, every decision boundary would pass through the origin.

questions.question: The vanishing gradient problem with sigmoid activations is caused by:

options.C: Their derivative being at most 0.25, shrinking gradients multiplied across layers

questions.explanation: Repeatedly multiplying factors < 1 makes early-layer gradients exponentially small.

questions.question: Which is a correct statement about the expressiveness guaranteed by the Universal Approximation Theorem?

options.B: It states existence of an approximating one-hidden-layer network, saying nothing about learnability or size efficiency
options.C: It applies only to linear functions
options.D: It guarantees a training algorithm will find the approximating weights

questions.explanation: Existence ≠ learnability: the theorem is silent on optimization and required width.

questions.question: Compared to a very wide shallow network, a deep network can represent certain functions:

options.B: With exponentially fewer units, thanks to feature reuse across layers

questions.explanation: Hierarchical composition lets deep nets reuse sub-features efficiently.

questions.question: In the layer abstraction used in the exercises, every layer implements:
questions.explanation: Composable forward/backward pairs are the building blocks of the framework.

questions.question: The gradient of the loss w.r.t. a layer's weights is computed in the backward pass using:

options.A: The incoming error tensor combined with the stored input of the forward pass

questions.explanation: That is why layers cache their inputs during forward.

questions.question: 'Fully connected' means that each neuron of a layer:

options.A: Connects only to one neuron of the previous layer
options.B: Receives input from every neuron of the previous layer

questions.explanation: Dense connectivity — hence the large parameter counts on images.

questions.question: Which pairing of task and standard loss is correct?
questions.explanation: MLE under Gaussian noise gives L2 for regression; under a categorical model it gives cross-entropy.

questions.question: Deriving the L2 loss from maximum likelihood assumes the regression targets are corrupted by:
questions.explanation: The negative log-likelihood of a Gaussian is the squared error up to constants.

questions.question: The softmax function turns a vector of raw scores (logits) into:

options.B: A probability distribution: positive values summing to 1

questions.explanation: exp-normalization keeps the ordering and yields class probabilities.

questions.question: The hinge loss max(0, 1 − y·f(x)) is historically associated with:

options.B: Support vector machines and maximum-margin classification

questions.explanation: It penalizes points inside the margin — the SVM objective's loss part.

questions.question: Why is optimizing accuracy directly with gradient descent not possible?

options.A: Accuracy is piecewise constant, so its gradient is zero almost everywhere

questions.explanation: Surrogate losses like cross-entropy provide useful gradients instead.

questions.question: The Adam optimizer maintains, per parameter:

options.B: Estimates of the first moment (mean) and second moment (uncentered variance) of the gradients, with bias correction

questions.explanation: Adaptive per-parameter step sizes come from the moment estimates.

questions.question: What does gradient clipping address?

options.D: Exploding gradients, by capping the gradient norm before the update

questions.explanation: Especially in RNNs, rescaling oversized gradients keeps updates stable.

questions.question: Compared to plain SGD, per-parameter adaptive methods (Adagrad/RMSprop) scale the step size by:

options.B: An accumulated (decayed) history of squared gradients

questions.explanation: Rarely-updated parameters get relatively larger steps.

questions.question: A typical learning-rate schedule over a long training run:

options.A: Starts higher for fast progress and decays (step, exponential, cosine) for fine convergence
options.D: Sets the rate to zero after one epoch

questions.explanation: Decay lets the optimizer settle into a minimum instead of bouncing around it.

questions.question: A batch size that is very small (e.g. 1) primarily leads to:

options.A: Noisy gradient estimates — which can even help escape sharp minima, but slows wall-clock convergence

questions.explanation: Noise acts like implicit regularization; large batches give smoother but costlier steps.

questions.question: The last activation and the loss are usually chosen TOGETHER because:

options.D: The loss assumes a particular output range/interpretation (e.g. probabilities for cross-entropy)

questions.explanation: Sigmoid+BCE and softmax+CE are matched pairs with clean gradients.

questions.question: The gradient of softmax combined with cross-entropy w.r.t. the logits simplifies to:

options.B: ŷ − y (predicted probabilities minus one-hot target)

questions.explanation: This elegant cancellation is a key reason the pair is used everywhere.

questions.question: Which observation signals CONVERGENCE of training?

options.A: Loss stops decreasing significantly and gradients become small

questions.explanation: Settling loss and vanishing updates indicate a (local) minimum was reached.

questions.question: What is the 'dying ReLU' problem?

options.A: Units stuck in the negative regime output 0 with zero gradient and stop learning permanently
options.D: ReLU cannot be computed on GPUs

questions.explanation: Once pre-activations stay negative, no gradient revives the unit — Leaky ReLU/ELU help.

questions.question: How does Leaky ReLU differ from ReLU?

options.A: It uses a small slope α·x instead of 0 for negative inputs

questions.explanation: The small negative slope keeps gradients alive for negative inputs.

questions.question: The ELU activation for x ≤ 0 computes:
questions.explanation: The smooth exponential saturation pushes mean activations toward zero.

questions.question: An advantage of tanh over sigmoid as hidden activation is that tanh:
questions.explanation: Zero-centered outputs avoid the systematic gradient sign coupling sigmoid causes.

questions.question: Output width of a convolution: input 32, kernel 5, stride 1, padding 2 gives:
Output width of a convolution: input 32, kernel 3, stride 2, padding 1 gives:
questions.explanation: (32 − 3 + 2)/2 + 1 = 16 — stride 2 halves the resolution.

questions.question: The receptive field of a unit deep in a CNN is:

options.B: The kernel size of its layer only
options.D: The region of the INPUT image that can influence that unit's activation

questions.explanation: Stacking layers (and striding/pooling) grows the effective input region per unit.

questions.question: Two stacked 3×3 convolutions (stride 1) have the same receptive field as one:

options.A: 5×5 — but with fewer parameters and an extra non-linearity

questions.explanation: The VGG insight: stacks of small kernels replace large ones efficiently.

questions.question: Convolutional feature extraction is approximately equivariant to:

options.B: Translation — shifting the input shifts the feature map correspondingly

questions.explanation: Weight sharing across positions produces the translation property.

questions.question: Average pooling differs from max pooling in that it:

options.A: Outputs the mean of the window instead of the maximum

questions.explanation: Both downsample; max keeps the strongest response, average smooths.

questions.question: How does the number of parameters of a conv layer depend on the input's spatial size?

options.D: Not at all — only kernel size and channel counts matter

questions.explanation: That independence lets one net process differently sized images.

questions.question: Global average pooling at the end of a CNN:

options.D: Collapses each feature map to a single value, enabling a fixed-size output for any input size

questions.explanation: It replaces flatten+FC heads and adds no parameters.

questions.question: A CONV layer has 64 kernels of size 5×5 on a 32-channel input. Total weights (no biases):
questions.explanation: kernels × input channels × kernel area.

questions.question: Which output activation yields the class probabilities of a 10-class classifier?
questions.explanation: Softmax normalizes the 10 logits into a distribution.

questions.question: Overfitting is best diagnosed by observing:

options.B: Training loss falling while validation loss rises

questions.explanation: The generalization gap opening up is the overfitting signature.

questions.question: Underfitting shows itself as:

options.A: Low training and low validation error
options.B: High error on BOTH training and validation data

questions.explanation: The model lacks capacity or training to even fit the training set.

questions.question: L2 regularization is equivalent to which optimizer-side technique?
questions.explanation: Shrinking weights each step by a factor implements the L2 penalty's gradient.

questions.question: From a Bayesian view, L2 regularization corresponds to which prior on the weights?
questions.explanation: L1 corresponds to a Laplacian prior — hence its sparsity.

questions.question: With inverted dropout (keep probability p), what happens at TEST time?

options.B: Units are dropped as in training
options.C: Nothing special — activations were already scaled by 1/p during training

questions.explanation: Scaling during training keeps expected activations consistent, so inference runs unchanged.

questions.question: Which of these is a form of data augmentation for images?

options.A: Random crops, flips and rotations of training images

questions.explanation: Label-preserving transforms multiply the effective training data.

questions.question: Early stopping works by:

options.C: Stopping when training loss is zero
options.D: Monitoring validation performance and keeping the best checkpoint before it degrades

questions.explanation: It restricts effective capacity by limiting optimization time.

questions.question: Batch normalization at TRAINING time normalizes each channel using:

options.B: The mean and variance computed over the current mini-batch, then applies learnable scale γ and shift β

questions.explanation: γ and β restore representational power after standardization.

questions.question: Why can dropout be interpreted as an implicit ensemble?

options.D: Each mask trains a different sub-network with shared weights; inference approximates averaging them

questions.explanation: Exponentially many thinned networks share parameters.

questions.question: Label noise and small datasets make which failure mode more likely?

options.C: Overfitting — the model memorizes noise instead of structure

questions.explanation: Regularization and augmentation are the standard countermeasures.

questions.question: In the Elman cell, the new hidden state is computed from:

options.C: The previous hidden state combined with the current input, passed through a non-linearity

questions.explanation: hₜ = f(W_hh·hₜ₋₁ + W_xh·xₜ) — recurrence in one line.

questions.question: Which mapping type describes machine translation (sentence in, sentence out)?
questions.explanation: Both sides are sequences of different lengths — the seq2seq setting.

questions.question: Image captioning (one image in, sentence out) is which mapping type?
questions.explanation: A single input unrolls into a generated sequence.

questions.question: Sentiment classification of a whole review (sequence in, one label out) is:
questions.explanation: The sequence is consumed and a single prediction emitted at the end.

questions.question: Backpropagation through time (BPTT) works by:

options.B: Unrolling the recurrence over the time steps and backpropagating through the unrolled graph

questions.explanation: The shared weights receive summed gradient contributions from every step.

questions.question: The LSTM forget gate:

options.C: Controls how much of the previous CELL STATE is kept

questions.explanation: A sigmoid gate scales cₜ₋₁ elementwise between 'erase' and 'keep'.

questions.question: The LSTM input gate together with the candidate values decides:

options.C: Which new information is written into the cell state

questions.explanation: Write control complements the forget gate's erase control.

questions.question: How does the GRU simplify the LSTM?

options.B: It merges cell and hidden state and uses two gates (update, reset) instead of three

questions.explanation: Fewer parameters, often comparable performance.

questions.question: A bidirectional RNN is appropriate when:

options.B: Only past context may be used (online prediction)
options.D: The whole sequence is available and future context helps, e.g. offline text tagging

questions.explanation: Two passes (forward and backward) are concatenated per time step.

questions.question: In seq2seq WITHOUT attention, the main information bottleneck is:

options.C: The single fixed-size context vector that must summarize the whole input sequence

questions.explanation: Attention was introduced precisely to let the decoder look back at all encoder states.

questions.question: Teacher forcing during sequence training means:

options.C: Feeding the ground-truth previous token as decoder input instead of the model's own prediction

questions.explanation: It stabilizes and speeds up training but creates exposure bias at inference.

questions.question: Greedy decoding vs. beam search: beam search

options.D: Keeps the k best partial sequences per step, trading computation for better overall sequence probability

questions.explanation: Greedy commits to the locally best token and can miss globally better sequences.

questions.question: The bottleneck (code) of an undercomplete autoencoder forces the network to:

options.A: Learn a compressed representation capturing the data's essential structure

questions.explanation: Reconstruction through fewer dimensions demands abstraction.

questions.question: A suitable loss for training an autoencoder on images is:

options.A: L2 (or L1) reconstruction loss between input and output

questions.explanation: The target is the input itself — no labels needed.

questions.question: An OVERcomplete autoencoder (code larger than input) risks:

options.C: Learning the identity mapping without extracting useful structure — unless regularized (e.g. sparsity, denoising)

questions.explanation: Extra capacity must be constrained to force meaningful codes.

questions.question: The VAE loss combines a reconstruction term with:

options.A: A KL-divergence term pulling the latent distribution toward the prior (e.g. standard Gaussian)

questions.explanation: The KL regularizer makes the latent space smooth and sampleable.

questions.question: The reparameterization trick in VAEs exists because:

options.B: Sampling z ~ N(μ,σ²) is not differentiable w.r.t. μ,σ; writing z = μ + σ·ε with ε ~ N(0,1) restores the gradient path

questions.explanation: Moving the randomness into ε makes μ and σ trainable by backprop.

questions.question: In a GAN, the generator receives as input:

options.A: A random noise vector (optionally plus a condition)

questions.explanation: It maps latent noise to synthetic samples.

questions.question: In a GAN, the discriminator's task is to:
