options.D: Classify whether a sample is real (from the dataset) or fake (from the generator)

questions.explanation: Its feedback is the training signal for the generator.

questions.question: A known GAN training failure where the generator maps many latent codes to few outputs is:
questions.explanation: Diversity-promoting techniques (minibatch discrimination, unrolled GANs) counteract it.

questions.question: Compared to GANs, VAEs typically produce:

options.B: Sharper images but without a latent space
options.D: Smoother/blurrier reconstructions but with a well-structured, easily sampleable latent space

questions.explanation: The Gaussian assumptions smooth outputs; GAN samples tend to be sharper.

questions.question: Which task is inherently UNsupervised?

options.A: Clustering images by similarity without any labels

questions.explanation: No annotations — the structure must come from the data itself.

questions.question: The three standard data splits and their roles are:

options.B: Train to fit, validation to tune hyperparameters/select models, test for the final unbiased evaluation

questions.explanation: Touching the test set during development invalidates the final estimate.

questions.question: Repeatedly tuning hyperparameters against the TEST set causes:
questions.explanation: The test set silently becomes a validation set — its estimate is no longer honest.

questions.question: Precision and recall are defined as:
questions.explanation: Precision: how many predicted positives are right; recall: how many actual positives were found.

questions.question: The F1 score is:

options.B: The arithmetic mean of precision and recall
options.C: The harmonic mean of precision and recall

questions.explanation: The harmonic mean punishes imbalance between the two.

questions.question: K-fold cross-validation is especially useful when:

options.C: Data is scarce and a single split would give a noisy performance estimate

questions.explanation: Rotating the held-out fold uses all data for both training and evaluation.

questions.question: Why does RANDOM search often beat GRID search for hyperparameters?

options.A: When only a few dimensions matter, random sampling covers each important axis with more distinct values

questions.explanation: Grid search wastes trials repeating values along unimportant axes.

questions.question: Fine-tuning differs from feature extraction (frozen backbone) in that fine-tuning:

options.B: Also updates (some) pretrained backbone weights with a small learning rate

questions.explanation: More adaptation, more risk of overfitting on small datasets.

questions.question: An ablation study serves to:

options.B: Measure each component's contribution by removing/altering it and re-evaluating

questions.explanation: It separates what actually helps from what merely tags along.

questions.question: AlexNet's 2012 ImageNet win popularized which combination?

options.D: ReLU activations, dropout and GPU training

questions.explanation: These ingredients made training a large CNN on ImageNet feasible.

questions.question: The 'degradation problem' motivating ResNet describes:

options.B: Deeper plain networks reaching HIGHER training error than shallower ones — an optimization, not overfitting, issue

questions.explanation: Identity shortcuts let extra layers default to a no-op, restoring trainability.

questions.question: A ResNet block computes:

options.C: y = F(x) + x, learning a residual on top of the identity

questions.explanation: Learning the residual F is easier than learning the full mapping.

questions.question: GoogLeNet's Inception module:

options.B: Runs 1×1, 3×3, 5×5 convolutions (and pooling) in parallel and concatenates the results

questions.explanation: 1×1 bottlenecks keep the parallel branches affordable.

questions.question: DenseNet's characteristic connectivity is:

options.D: Each layer receives the concatenated feature maps of ALL preceding layers in its block

questions.explanation: Maximal feature reuse with relatively few parameters per layer.

questions.question: MobileNet reduces computation mainly through:

options.D: Depthwise separable convolutions splitting spatial filtering and channel mixing

questions.explanation: A depthwise conv per channel followed by 1×1 pointwise convs approximates a full conv cheaply.

questions.question: The Vision Transformer (ViT) feeds images to a transformer by:

options.D: Splitting the image into fixed-size patches, embedding each as a token (plus position information)

questions.explanation: Patch embeddings make self-attention over image regions tractable.

questions.question: Occlusion-based visualization works by:

options.B: Sliding a mask over the input and recording how the class score drops

questions.explanation: Score-sensitive regions reveal what the classifier relies on.

questions.question: Grad-CAM produces its heatmap from:

options.A: Gradient-weighted activation maps of a late convolutional layer

questions.explanation: Channel importance (via gradients) weights the spatial activations — coarse but class-discriminative.

questions.question: t-SNE is typically used to:

options.B: Visualize high-dimensional embeddings in 2-D while preserving local neighborhoods

questions.explanation: A go-to tool for inspecting learned feature spaces.

questions.question: In scaled dot-product attention, the attention weights are computed as:
questions.explanation: Similarity of queries and keys, scaled and normalized, mixes the values.

questions.question: Multi-head attention improves on a single head by:

options.B: Attending in several learned subspaces in parallel, capturing different relationship types

questions.explanation: Heads specialize (syntax, position, semantics, ...) and are concatenated.

questions.question: Why do transformers need positional encoding?

options.D: Because self-attention is permutation-invariant and otherwise ignores token order

questions.explanation: Position information must be injected explicitly into the embeddings.

questions.question: DeepDream-style visualizations are created by:

options.D: Optimizing the INPUT image via gradient ascent to maximize chosen activations, with regularization

questions.explanation: The network's preferences are made visible in the input space.

questions.question: The discount factor γ in the return Σ γᵏ rₜ₊ₖ controls:

options.D: How strongly future rewards count relative to immediate ones

questions.explanation: γ close to 1: far-sighted agent; γ near 0: myopic agent.

questions.question: A policy π in reinforcement learning is:

options.D: A mapping from states to (probabilities of) actions

questions.explanation: It defines the agent's behavior in every state.

questions.question: The action-value function Q(s,a) estimates:

options.B: The expected return of taking action a in state s and following the policy afterwards

questions.explanation: Q-values let the agent rank actions per state.

questions.question: The Bellman equation expresses the value of a state as:

options.C: The immediate reward plus the discounted value of the successor state(s)

questions.explanation: This recursive consistency underlies value iteration and Q-learning.

questions.question: In Deep Q-Networks (DQN), experience replay serves to:

options.B: Break the correlation of consecutive transitions by sampling past experiences randomly

questions.explanation: Decorrelated batches make Q-learning with neural networks stable.

questions.question: The target network in DQN:

options.C: Provides slowly-updated target values, preventing the moving-target instability

questions.explanation: Bootstrapped targets from a frozen copy stabilize training.

questions.question: Actor-critic methods combine:

options.C: A policy network (actor) with a value network (critic) that evaluates the actor's actions

questions.explanation: The critic's value estimate reduces the variance of the policy gradient.

questions.question: Why does U-Net feed encoder features to the decoder via skip connections?

options.B: To recover fine spatial detail lost through pooling, enabling precise boundaries

questions.explanation: High-resolution features from early layers sharpen the upsampled predictions.

questions.question: Transposed ('up') convolutions are used in segmentation networks to:

options.D: Learn upsampling back to the input resolution

questions.explanation: The decoder must undo the encoder's downsampling to label every pixel.

questions.question: Panoptic segmentation combines:
questions.explanation: Every pixel gets a class AND, where applicable, an instance identity.

questions.question: Non-maximum suppression (NMS) in object detection:

options.B: Removes overlapping duplicate detections, keeping the highest-scoring box per object

questions.explanation: Detectors propose many boxes per object; NMS keeps one.

questions.question: A detection counts as correct at IoU threshold 0.5 when:

options.A: The predicted box overlaps the ground-truth box with IoU ≥ 0.5 and the class matches
options.B: The class is right, regardless of the box

questions.explanation: IoU gates localization quality; the threshold defines 'good enough'.

questions.question: Mask R-CNN extends Faster R-CNN by:

options.C: Adding a parallel branch that predicts a segmentation mask for each detected instance

questions.explanation: Detection boxes plus per-instance masks = instance segmentation.

questions.question: In YOLO, the image is divided into a grid where each cell:

options.A: Is classified independently as one pixel
options.D: Predicts bounding boxes with confidences and class probabilities directly

questions.explanation: Direct dense prediction is what makes YOLO fast.

questions.question: Why is it useful to learn a bias term in addition to the weights?

options.B: It guarantees the activations stay positive
options.C: It prevents the weights from growing too large
options.D: It shifts the activation function, so the decision boundary need not pass through the origin

questions.explanation: Without a bias, every layer computes a purely linear map through the origin, which severely restricts the functions the network can represent.

questions.question: What does the model capacity of a network describe?

options.A: The amount of GPU memory it occupies
options.B: The maximum batch size it can process
options.C: The number of training samples it was optimised with
options.D: The variety of functions it can approximate

questions.explanation: Capacity is a property of the model class, not of the dataset; it grows with depth and width and sits at the centre of the bias-variance trade-off.

questions.question: Which statements about model capacity are TRUE? (Select all that apply)

options.A: It is linked to the variety of functions that can be approximated
options.B: It is influenced by the depth of the network
options.C: Increasing capacity necessarily increases the bias of the prediction
options.D: It is closely related to the bias-variance trade-off

questions.explanation: Higher capacity typically lowers bias and raises variance, so the last statement has the relationship backwards.

questions.question: You feed a network trained on ImageNet a blank image where every pixel has the same white value. Will it output the same score for every class?

options.A: It cannot be determined without knowing the architecture
options.B: Yes, because a constant input carries no class information
options.C: No, because the weights and biases differ per class, so a constant input still produces different scores
options.D: Yes, but only if the last layer is a SoftMax

questions.explanation: A constant input is still multiplied by different weight vectors, so the logits differ; uniform output would require identical weights per class.

questions.question: In a network with two fully connected layers and no non-linearity between them, why is the model no more expressive than a single layer?

options.B: Because the composition of two linear maps is again a linear map, W = W2 W1 with bias W2 b1 + b2
options.C: Because the second layer's weights are always zero

questions.explanation: Only a non-linearity between the layers lets the network represent functions a single affine map cannot.

questions.question: Data normalisation example: a feature has mean 82 and standard deviation 40, and the observed value is 22. What is the normalised value?
questions.explanation: Standardisation subtracts the mean and divides by the standard deviation: (22 - 82) / 40 = -1.5.

questions.question: A two-feature dataset is normalised to (-1, -1), (-1, 1), (1, -1), (1, 1) with alternating labels along the diagonals. Was a single-layer perceptron a good choice?

options.A: No, because the data must first be augmented
options.B: Yes, because normalisation always makes data separable
options.C: Yes, the data is linearly separable after normalisation
options.D: No, the distribution is an XOR problem, which a single-layer perceptron cannot solve

questions.explanation: XOR requires at least one hidden layer; no single hyperplane separates the two diagonal pairs.

questions.question: How do you connect the output of a convolutional layer to the input of a fully connected layer?

options.A: By adding a second convolutional layer with kernel size equal to the image size
options.B: By averaging all channels into a single scalar
options.C: By inserting a flatten layer that reshapes the feature maps into a vector

questions.explanation: Flattening (or global pooling) turns the multi-dimensional feature maps into the one-dimensional vector the fully connected layer expects.

questions.question: A network flattens a feature map and applies a fully connected layer. A colleague replaces this with a convolution whose kernel covers the whole feature map. How do the two compare?

options.A: The convolution cannot produce the same output
options.B: They are equivalent; the convolutional version avoids the explicit flatten and may be implemented more efficiently
options.C: The convolution has far fewer parameters
options.D: The fully connected version is translation invariant, the convolutional one is not

questions.explanation: A convolution whose kernel spans the entire input is exactly a fully connected layer written in convolutional form.

questions.question: An image is (X, X) and a fully connected layer produces Z outputs. Ignoring the bias, how many weights does it need?
questions.explanation: Every one of the X^2 input pixels connects to every one of the Z output neurons.

questions.question: The same image is processed by a convolutional layer with N kernels of size (K, K) on a single input channel. Ignoring bias, how many weights does it need?
questions.explanation: Weight sharing makes the parameter count independent of the image size, which is the key advantage over the fully connected layer.

questions.question: For a 2-D convolution with an EVEN filter width k and stride 1, what padding gives an output of the same size as the input?

options.C: Asymmetric padding: k/2 - 1 on one side and k/2 on the other
options.D: Same size output is impossible for even k

questions.explanation: Odd kernels allow the symmetric (k-1)/2; an even kernel has no centre pixel, so the two sides must differ by one.

questions.question: Which statement about a 'valid' convolution is FALSE?

options.A: It produces a smaller output than a 'same' convolution with the same kernel
options.C: It outputs an image of the same size as its input
options.D: It discards border positions where the kernel does not fully overlap the input

questions.explanation: That describes 'same' convolution. A valid convolution shrinks the output by k-1 in each spatial dimension.

questions.question: A CONV layer has 16 kernels applied to an input with 3 channels. How many channels does the output have?
questions.explanation: Each kernel spans all input channels and produces exactly one output feature map, so the number of kernels sets the output depth.

questions.question: A pytorch model applies nn.Conv2d(3, 16, 3, padding=1) to an input of shape 256 x 256 x 3, and later maps back to 3 channels with the same padding. What is the output shape?
questions.explanation: A 3x3 kernel with padding 1 and stride 1 preserves the spatial size, so only the channel count changes through the network.

questions.question: Why is a stride greater than 1 sometimes described as subsampling?

options.A: Because it applies the kernel to a random subset of positions
options.B: Because it reduces the number of channels
options.C: Because it evaluates the convolution only at every s-th position, discarding the intermediate outputs

questions.explanation: That equivalence is exactly why a strided convolution can be implemented as a stride-1 convolution followed by dropping rows and columns.

questions.question: A strided convolution is implemented as stride-1 convolution plus subsampling. What must happen to the error tensor in the backward pass?

options.A: It must be averaged over the stride window
options.B: It must be scaled by the stride
options.D: It must be padded with zeros at the rows and columns that were discarded in the forward pass

questions.explanation: Positions that produced no output receive no gradient, so inserting zeros restores the shape the stride-1 backward pass expects.

questions.question: Name a plausible cause of internal covariate shift.

options.A: The distribution of a layer's inputs changes as the parameters of the preceding layers are updated
options.C: The test set is drawn from a different population than the training set
options.D: The learning rate is too small

questions.explanation: Because every layer's input is the previous layer's output, updating early layers shifts the distribution that later layers have to cope with.

questions.question: Which situations contribute to internal covariate shift? (Select all that apply)

options.A: Large learning rates causing large parameter changes per step
options.B: Unnormalised input data with widely differing feature scales
options.C: Weight updates in earlier layers changing the distribution seen by later layers

questions.explanation: The seed only fixes the initialisation and shuffling order; it does not affect how distributions drift during training.

questions.question: You train one network with L2 regularization and one with L1. How do the weights differ from an unregularised run?

options.B: L2 gives sparser weights, L1 gives uniformly smaller weights
options.C: L2 gives smaller weights overall, L1 gives sparser weights with many close to zero
options.D: L1 increases the weights, L2 decreases them

questions.explanation: The quadratic penalty shrinks large weights hardest, whereas the absolute-value penalty has constant gradient magnitude and drives small weights to exactly zero.

questions.question: Which observation about a trained weight histogram most strongly suggests L1 rather than L2 regularization?

options.A: A narrow Gaussian centred at zero
options.B: A bimodal distribution with peaks at -1 and 1
options.D: A sharp spike exactly at zero with a spread of non-zero weights around it

questions.explanation: L2 shrinks everything smoothly towards zero, so it yields a narrow bell shape rather than an atom of mass exactly at zero.

questions.question: Three loss-over-iteration curves are observed for the same task, with noise levels high, medium and almost none. Which ordering matches SGD, mini-batch GD and batch GD?

options.C: Batch GD is the noisiest, SGD the smoothest
options.D: SGD is the noisiest, mini-batch is intermediate, batch GD is the smoothest

questions.explanation: The gradient estimate averages over more samples as the batch grows, so its variance and therefore the curve noise shrink.

questions.question: A GAN is trained with mini-batches in which all dog images come first, then all cat images. Why is this a problem?

options.A: Each mini-batch gradient is biased towards one class, so the updates oscillate instead of approximating the full-data gradient
options.B: The discriminator cannot process two classes
options.C: It is not a problem as long as every image is seen once per epoch

questions.explanation: Mini-batch gradient descent assumes each batch is a representative sample; sorted batches break that assumption badly.

questions.question: Why does a classifier that ends with ReLU followed by sigmoid, thresholded at 0.5, classify every sample as positive?

options.A: Because the ReLU output is always >= 0, so the sigmoid output is always >= 0.5
options.C: Because the loss function is wrong
options.D: Because the weights were initialised with zeros

questions.explanation: sigmoid(0) = 0.5 and the function is monotonic, so a non-negative pre-activation can never fall below the threshold.

questions.question: Would a step-like activation that is 0 for x < -0.5, x on [-0.5, 0.5] and 1 for x > 0.5 train well with SGD?

options.A: Yes, it behaves like a bounded ReLU
options.B: No, because it is not continuous
options.D: No, because the gradient is zero almost everywhere outside the narrow linear region

questions.explanation: Backpropagation multiplies by the derivative, so units driven into the flat regions stop receiving any learning signal.

questions.question: A simplified Elman cell has h_t = ReLU(w_hh * h_{t-1} + w_xh * x_t) with w_hh = 1, w_xh = 2, h_0 = 0 and x_1 = 2. What is h_1?
questions.explanation: The recurrent contribution is zero at t = 1, so h_1 = ReLU(2 * 2) = 4.

questions.question: Continuing that cell with x_2 = -1, w_hh = 1, w_xh = 2 and h_1 = 4, what is h_2?
questions.explanation: h_2 = ReLU(1 * 4 + 2 * (-1)) = ReLU(2) = 2, so the ReLU does not clip in this step.

questions.question: With w_hy = 0.5 and an output y_t = ReLU(w_hy * h_t), what is y_1 when h_1 = 4?
In a recurrent cell with only ReLU non-linearities, all inputs positive and all weights positive, what goes wrong for long sequences, and how does a standard Elman cell differ?

options.A: Nothing goes wrong; ReLU cells are preferred for long sequences
options.B: Exploding gradients; a standard Elman cell passes the state through tanh, which bounds it and instead risks vanishing gradients
options.C: Vanishing gradients; the standard cell is worse because tanh saturates
options.D: The hidden state becomes constant; the standard cell has the same issue

questions.explanation: With no bounded non-linearity, repeated multiplication by weights greater than one makes the state and its gradient grow without limit.

questions.question: How would you fix a recurrent cell that suffers from exploding activations because all weights and inputs are positive?

options.D: Use a bounded activation such as tanh or sigmoid, or initialise with a zero-centred distribution

questions.explanation: Either bound the signal so it cannot grow, or make positive and negative contributions cancel on average.

questions.question: Why are recurrent neural networks suitable for time-series problems?

options.A: Because the hidden state carries information from earlier time steps, capturing temporal correlation
options.C: Because they process the whole sequence in parallel
options.D: Because they need fewer parameters than fully connected networks

questions.explanation: Neighbouring samples in a time series are correlated, and the recurrence is exactly the mechanism that lets earlier samples influence later predictions.

questions.question: What is the main benefit of an LSTM compared to an Elman cell?

options.A: It does not require backpropagation through time
options.B: Its gated cell state preserves gradients over long sequences, mitigating the vanishing gradient problem
options.C: It can process variable-length sequences, which the Elman cell cannot

questions.explanation: The additive cell state gives gradients a route through time that is not repeatedly squashed by a saturating non-linearity.

questions.question: An architecture that maps an input to a compressed representation and reconstructs it is used for self-representation learning. What is it called?
questions.explanation: Training targets the input itself, which makes it unsupervised, and the bottleneck forces a compact representation.

questions.question: How does a conditional GAN let a user request a specific kind of generated image?

options.A: By using a larger noise vector
options.B: By giving both generator and discriminator an additional input that encodes the target class
options.C: By filtering the generated samples after training
options.D: By retraining the generator for each request

questions.explanation: The training set must then contain labelled examples of each target category so the discriminator can learn the conditional distribution.

questions.question: Kernel weights are hard to interpret. What do you visualise instead to understand a convolutional layer?

options.B: The feature maps, i.e. the activations the layer produces for a given input

questions.explanation: Activations show what the layer actually responds to for a concrete image, which is far more interpretable than the raw filter coefficients.

questions.question: Name a measure that can evaluate segmentation but not plain classification.
questions.explanation: IoU and the Dice coefficient compare predicted and true regions, which only makes sense when the prediction has spatial extent.

questions.question: Which statement about semantic segmentation is CORRECT?

options.A: It treats multiple objects of the same class as separate entities
options.B: It draws a bounding box around the object of interest
options.C: It can only find one object per image
options.D: It can differentiate between different classes in an image, assigning a class label per pixel

questions.explanation: Distinguishing separate objects of the same class is instance segmentation; bounding boxes are object detection.

questions.question: Can a pixel-wise segmentation be converted into a bounding box?

options.A: Only if the segmentation is binary
options.C: No, the two representations are incompatible
options.D: Yes, by taking the extent of the segmented region

questions.explanation: The conversion loses information and does not work in the other direction, which is why segmentation labels are the richer annotation.

questions.question: You must count the people in a room to check it is below capacity. Which task fits best?

options.A: Semantic segmentation, since it labels every person pixel
options.B: Image classification, since the answer is a single number
options.C: Denoising, since the images are noisy
options.D: Object detection, since it produces one instance per person and instances can be counted

questions.explanation: Semantic segmentation merges all people into one class region, so overlapping people cannot be told apart to be counted.

questions.question: A U-Net-like model is missing one key concept compared to the real U-Net. Which one?

options.A: Skip connections from the encoder to the decoder

questions.explanation: Those connections carry high-resolution spatial detail that the downsampling path would otherwise discard.

questions.question: Why is classifying each pixel independently with fully connected layers a poor approach to segmentation? (Select all that apply)

options.A: Fully connected layers cannot produce more than two output classes
options.B: The approach discards the spatial context around each pixel
options.C: The number of parameters is far larger than for a convolutional network
options.D: Fully connected layers are not translation invariant, so an object at a new location is harder to segment

questions.explanation: The class count is not the issue; the problems are the loss of weight sharing, the parameter explosion and the missing spatial context.

questions.question: In which scenario would you use under- or oversampling?

options.B: To counter class imbalance during training
options.C: Inside the dropout layer to boost the influence of certain weights
options.D: To create artificial data with variational autoencoders

questions.explanation: Undersampling removes majority samples and oversampling repeats minority samples so the classes contribute more equally to the loss.

questions.question: How should the FINAL performance of a fine-tuned network be evaluated?

options.A: A metric such as an ROC curve computed on the held-out test set
options.D: Mean precision over training, validation and test data combined

questions.explanation: The validation set guided model selection, so it is no longer unbiased; only untouched test data gives an honest estimate.

questions.question: A network trained on 32x32 images ends with flatten and fully connected layers. How do you adapt it to accept arbitrarily sized images and still output a vector of length 10?

options.B: Replace the flatten and fully connected layers with convolutional layers and average pooling
options.C: Add a recurrent layer at the end
options.D: Retrain the whole network from scratch

questions.explanation: Convolutions have no fixed input size, and global average pooling collapses whatever spatial extent remains into a fixed-length vector.

questions.question: In exercise 0 the pattern classes (Checker, Circle, Spectrum) must be built without Python loops. Why?

options.A: The unit tests inspect the source code for the word 'for'
options.B: Python-level loops are slow, so vectorised NumPy operations are required
options.C: NumPy arrays cannot be indexed inside a loop
options.D: Loops would produce mathematically wrong patterns

questions.explanation: Python is interpreted, so element-wise loops are far slower than vectorised NumPy calls. The exercise forces you to learn proper indexing and slicing.

questions.question: Which three methods does every pattern class in exercise 0 provide?
questions.explanation: Each pattern is a class with a constructor, a draw() that builds the array into the instance variable output, and a show() that displays it.

questions.question: The Checker class must reject some resolutions. Which condition does a valid checkerboard resolution satisfy?

options.A: resolution must be evenly divisible by 2 * tile_size
options.B: resolution must be evenly divisible by tile_size only
options.C: resolution must be larger than tile_size squared
options.D: resolution must be a power of two

questions.explanation: A checker period is two tiles wide, so only multiples of 2*tile_size tile the image without a truncated block at the border.

questions.question: In the Checker pattern of exercise 0, which tile must be black?

options.A: The tile in the bottom right corner
options.B: The tile in the top left corner
options.C: It does not matter, the tests accept both phases

questions.explanation: The description fixes the phase of the pattern by requiring the top-left tile to be black; otherwise the reference array comparison fails.

questions.question: draw() stores the pattern in the instance variable output. What should it return?

options.A: None, the caller reads output directly
options.B: A reference to output, so callers can modify it in place

questions.explanation: Returning a copy prevents a caller from mutating the stored pattern through the returned reference.

questions.question: The Circle class receives resolution, radius and position. What does draw() produce?

options.A: A filled square inscribed in the circle
options.B: A binary image of a circle at the given centre
options.C: A ring outline one pixel wide
options.D: A grayscale circle with a smooth intensity falloff

questions.explanation: The exercise asks for a binary circle drawn with NumPy operations; a single library call that draws circles is explicitly not accepted.

questions.question: The Spectrum class of exercise 0 produces an RGB image. What is the intensity range of each channel?
questions.explanation: Each of the three channels rises across a different spatial direction, with 0.0 as the minimum and 1.0 as the maximum.

questions.question: What does ImageGenerator.next() return?

options.A: Only the labels for the next batch
options.B: A tuple (images, labels) forming one batch
options.C: A generator object that must be iterated

questions.explanation: next() hands back one batch as a tuple of the image array and the matching label array.

questions.question: In exercise 0, what must the ImageGenerator do when the last batch of an epoch is smaller than batch_size?

options.B: Return the short batch as is
options.D: Fill it up by reusing images from the beginning of the dataset

questions.explanation: All batches must have the same size, so the tail batch is completed by wrapping around to the start of the data.

questions.question: With shuffle=True, what must the ImageGenerator guarantee within one epoch?

options.B: The order inside each batch is random but the batch order is fixed
options.D: No duplicates appear within one epoch, and the indices are reshuffled after each epoch

questions.explanation: Shuffling must randomise the whole dataset order, not just within a batch, and must not return duplicates before the epoch ends.

questions.question: How are the rotation and mirroring augmentations applied in ImageGenerator.next()?

options.A: Only during the second and later epochs
options.B: On an image-by-image basis, so a batch can mix transformed and untransformed images
options.C: Only to the first image of every batch
options.D: To the whole batch at once, so all images share the same transform

questions.explanation: The description states that augmentation is per image, which is why a single batch may contain rotated and non-rotated images side by side.

questions.question: Which rotation angles does the ImageGenerator's rotation flag apply?

options.B: Any angle drawn uniformly from 0 to 360 degrees

questions.explanation: Only multiples of 90 degrees are used, which keeps the image grid intact and needs no interpolation.

questions.question: In exercise 0, what is the difference between resizing and reshaping an image?

options.A: They are two names for the same operation
options.B: Resizing interpolates the data, reshaping only reorders the existing values
options.C: Resizing works on colour images, reshaping only on grayscale
options.D: Reshaping changes the number of pixels, resizing does not

questions.explanation: The description explicitly warns against confusing the two: resize interpolates (e.g. skimage.transform.resize), reshape merely reinterprets the same buffer.

questions.question: How are the labels stored for the exercise 0 image generator?

options.A: As one-hot vectors inside each .npy file
options.B: In a JSON file mapping the image filename to an integer class label
options.C: As a CSV table with one row per image
options.D: Encoded in the image filenames themselves

questions.explanation: The JSON is a dictionary whose key is the filename (e.g. '15' for 15.npy) and whose value is the integer class label.

questions.question: What does ImageGenerator.current_epoch() return, and when does it change?

options.A: The number of batches served so far, incremented on every next() call
options.B: The current epoch number, updated in next() when iteration restarts from the beginning of the dataset
options.C: The remaining epochs until training finishes
options.D: A float between 0 and 1 giving progress through the epoch

questions.explanation: The counter tracks complete passes through the data and is advanced inside next() when the index wraps around.

questions.question: How do you run only one specific test case from the exercise 0 test suite?

options.B: You cannot; the suite always runs completely

questions.explanation: Passing the test name as a command line parameter runs that single test; passing nothing runs all of them.

questions.question: How are bonus points for an exercise computed automatically?

options.A: By counting the lines of code in the submission
options.B: By running the test suite with the Bonus command line flag
options.D: By measuring the runtime of the unit tests

questions.explanation: Running the test file with 'Bonus' as the parameter reports the bonus points achieved for that exercise.

questions.question: What is the purpose of dispatch.py in the exercise material?

options.A: It dispatches gradients to the optimizer
options.B: It trains the network on a compute cluster
options.D: It checks the submission for completeness and zips the files needed for upload

questions.explanation: dispatch.py packages src_to_implement into a submission zip after checking that the required files are present.

questions.question: Which two methods must every layer in the exercise framework implement?
questions.explanation: The layer-oriented framework is built around exactly these two operations, executed during training and testing.

questions.question: What distinguishes a trainable from a non-trainable layer in the framework?

options.A: Trainable layers have parameters optimised during training; the inherited member 'trainable' is set to True
options.B: Trainable layers implement backward(), non-trainable ones do not
options.C: Trainable layers must be the last layers of the network
options.D: Non-trainable layers are skipped in the forward pass

questions.explanation: BaseLayer initialises trainable=False; layers with parameters such as FullyConnected set it to True so the network knows to give them an optimizer.

questions.question: In BaseLayer, what is the default value of the member 'trainable'?

options.B: It is not initialised in the constructor

questions.explanation: The base constructor sets trainable to False; only layers with parameters override it.

questions.question: Why does the course build a layer-oriented framework rather than a graph-oriented one?

options.A: Layer-oriented frameworks are strictly more flexible
options.B: It offers a higher level of abstraction: less flexibility, but easy experimentation with conventional architectures
options.D: Layer-oriented frameworks avoid the need for a loss function

questions.explanation: The trade-off is deliberate: stacking elementary layers limits the expressible graphs but keeps the implementation simple.

questions.question: What does the Sgd constructor receive in exercise 1?

options.A: The learning rate as a float
options.B: Nothing; the learning rate is passed to calculate_update
options.C: The learning rate and the momentum rate

questions.explanation: Plain SGD only needs a learning rate; momentum and Adam arrive in exercise 2 with additional constructor arguments.

questions.question: What is the signature of the optimizer method used to update parameters?
questions.explanation: Every optimizer in the framework exposes calculate_update(weight_tensor, gradient_tensor) and returns the updated weights.

questions.question: In the FullyConnected layer, what is the layout of input_tensor?

options.B: A 4-D tensor in b, c, y, x order
options.D: A flat 1-D vector of length input_size * batch_size

questions.explanation: Rows index the batch, columns index the features, so the layer computes X W with X of shape (batch_size, input_size).

questions.question: How does the exercise framework handle the bias of a FullyConnected layer?

options.A: It is folded into the weights matrix, so a single matrix multiplication suffices
options.B: The bias is applied by the optimizer, not the layer
options.C: It is stored in a separate member and added after the matrix multiplication
options.D: The bias is not learned at all

questions.explanation: Appending a constant 1 to the input and an extra row to the weights turns the affine map into one matrix product.

questions.question: How are the weights of the FullyConnected layer initialised in exercise 1, before Initializers exist?
questions.explanation: Exercise 1 uses a simple uniform random initialisation; Xavier and He are added in exercise 2.

questions.question: Which property must FullyConnected expose so the unit tests can check the parameter gradient?
questions.explanation: The tests access the property gradient_weights after the backward pass; the parameters themselves live in a member called weights.

questions.question: When should the FullyConnected layer skip the weight update in its backward pass?

options.A: When no optimizer has been set on the layer
options.B: When the error tensor is all zeros
options.C: It should never skip the update

questions.explanation: The layer calls calculate_update only if its optimizer property has been assigned; otherwise it just propagates the error.

questions.question: The FullyConnected backward pass needs a quantity that is not passed to backward(). Which one, and how is it obtained?

options.A: The input tensor of the forward pass; it must be stored during forward()
options.B: The output of the next layer; it is recomputed
options.C: The learning rate; it is read from the optimizer
options.D: The label tensor; it is fetched from the data layer

questions.explanation: The weight gradient is the product of the stored input and the incoming error, so the forward pass must cache its input.

questions.question: Why is the ReLU described as having 'revolutionised' neural networks in the exercise slides?

options.A: It reduces the effect of the vanishing gradient problem
options.B: It removes the need for a loss function
options.C: It makes the network invariant to translation

questions.explanation: Unlike saturating activations, ReLU has gradient 1 on the positive side, so gradients do not shrink layer after layer.

questions.question: Does the ReLU layer in the exercise framework set trainable to True?

options.A: Only when an optimizer is attached
options.B: Yes, but only in the convolutional variant
options.C: Yes, it learns a slope parameter
options.D: No, it has no trainable parameters so the inherited default is kept

questions.explanation: ReLU is a fixed non-linearity; the constructor takes no arguments and leaves trainable at False.

questions.question: What does the SoftMax layer do in the exercise framework?

options.A: It normalises the weights of the previous layer
options.B: It computes the loss value directly
options.C: It clips the logits to the range [0, 1]
options.D: It transforms the logits into a probability distribution, one per row of the batch

questions.explanation: Each row of the batch is turned into estimated class probabilities, which is why SoftMax is the usual final layer for classification.

questions.question: Which class in exercise 1 does NOT inherit from BaseLayer?
questions.explanation: The loss is deliberately not treated as a layer in this framework, so CrossEntropyLoss stands outside the layer hierarchy.

questions.question: What arguments do the forward and backward methods of CrossEntropyLoss take?
questions.explanation: Backpropagation starts at the loss, so no incoming error tensor exists; the labels are needed instead.

questions.question: What does CrossEntropyLoss.forward return?

options.A: The gradient with respect to the predictions
options.B: The loss accumulated over the batch
options.C: A vector with one loss value per sample

questions.explanation: The forward pass sums the per-sample cross entropy over the batch into a single scalar loss.

questions.question: Which five members does the NeuralNetwork class of exercise 1 hold?
questions.explanation: The optimizer arrives on construction, loss collects the per-iteration values, layers holds the architecture, and the two remaining members reference the data source and the loss layer.

questions.question: How does the NeuralNetwork obtain training data?

options.A: It reads a CSV file directly
options.B: Each layer fetches its own data
options.C: By calling next() on its data_layer, which returns an input tensor and a label tensor
options.D: The data is passed to the constructor

questions.explanation: The data layer is a generator-style object; next() yields the pair used for one iteration.

questions.question: In append_layer(layer), why is a deep copy of the network's optimizer made for each trainable layer?

options.B: To allow different learning rates per layer by default
options.C: Because optimizers such as Adam keep internal state that must not be shared between layers
options.D: Because Python cannot pass objects by reference

questions.explanation: Momentum and Adam accumulate per-parameter moments; sharing one object across layers would mix up their states.

questions.question: What does NeuralNetwork.test(input_tensor) return?

options.A: The prediction of the last layer, typically the probabilistic SoftMax output
options.B: The gradient with respect to the input
options.D: The loss on the given input

questions.explanation: test() only runs the forward pass through the layers and returns the network output; the loss layer is not involved.

questions.question: What does NeuralNetwork.train(iterations) store?

options.B: The weights of every layer at every step
options.C: The loss value for each iteration in the member 'loss'
options.D: A checkpoint file after every iteration

questions.explanation: train() runs forward and backward for the given number of iterations and appends each loss to the list.

questions.question: In which order does NeuralNetwork.backward() traverse the network?

options.A: From the loss layer backwards through the layers, passing the label tensor in first
options.B: Trainable layers first, then non-trainable ones
options.C: From the data layer forwards to the loss layer
options.D: In the order layers were appended

questions.explanation: Backpropagation starts at the loss with the current label tensor and hands each layer's returned error tensor to its predecessor.

questions.question: Which four initializer classes are implemented in exercise 2?
questions.explanation: All four expose initialize(weights_shape, fan_in, fan_out) and return a tensor of the requested shape.

questions.question: What is the default constant value of the Constant initializer?
questions.explanation: The class takes the value as a constructor argument with 0.1 as the default.

questions.question: What is the common signature of every initializer's initialize method?
questions.explanation: A uniform signature lets NeuralNetwork.append_layer initialise any trainable layer without knowing its type.

questions.question: For a fully connected layer, how are fan_in and fan_out defined?

options.A: fan_in is the number of samples, fan_out the number of classes
options.B: fan_in is the input dimension of the weights, fan_out the output dimension
options.C: fan_in is the batch size, fan_out the number of layers
options.D: Both equal the number of weights

questions.explanation: They are simply the two dimensions of the weight matrix.

questions.question: For a convolutional layer, how is fan_in defined in the exercise slides?

options.A: kernel height x kernel width only
options.B: number of output channels x kernel height x kernel width
options.D: number of input channels x kernel height x kernel width

questions.explanation: fan_in counts every weight feeding one output unit; fan_out uses the number of output channels with the same kernel extent.

questions.question: Xavier/Glorot initialisation draws weights from a zero-mean Gaussian. What is the standard deviation?
questions.explanation: Xavier balances the variance of the forward and the backward signal, so both fan_in and fan_out appear.

questions.question: He initialisation differs from Xavier in which way?

options.A: It initialises the bias instead of the weights
options.B: It uses a uniform rather than a Gaussian distribution
options.C: It scales by the batch size
options.D: Its standard deviation is sqrt(2 / fan_in), i.e. determined by the previous layer size only

questions.explanation: He was derived from Xavier specifically for ReLU activations, which discard the negative half of the signal.

questions.question: In exercise 2, how is the bias of a FullyConnected layer initialised?

options.A: It is not initialised at all
options.B: With the same initializer as the weights
options.C: Always with zeros, ignoring any initializer

questions.explanation: initialize(weights_initializer, bias_initializer) treats the bias separately even though it is stored inside the weights matrix.

questions.question: What arguments does the SgdWithMomentum constructor receive, in order?
questions.explanation: The order matters because the unit tests construct the optimizer positionally.

questions.question: What arguments does the Adam constructor receive, in order?
questions.explanation: In the literature mu is usually called beta1 and rho beta2.

questions.question: Which values are given as common defaults for Adam in the exercise slides?
questions.explanation: These are the standard Adam hyperparameters; mu weights the first moment and rho the second.

questions.question: Which momentum values are listed as common in the exercise slides?
questions.explanation: Momentum close to but below 1 accumulates past gradients over roughly 1/(1-mu) steps.

questions.question: Adam applies bias correction with terms 1 - mu^k and 1 - rho^k. What is k here?

options.C: An exponent, not an iteration index

questions.explanation: The exercise slides flag this explicitly: k is used as an exponent so the correction decays as training progresses.

questions.question: Why does Adam need bias correction at all?

options.A: Because the learning rate is too large at the start
options.C: Because the bias term of each layer is otherwise ignored
options.D: Because the moment estimates are initialised at zero and would otherwise be biased towards zero early in training

questions.explanation: With v and r starting at zero, the raw running averages underestimate the true moments in the first iterations.

questions.question: How does the exercise framework store optimizer state such as the momentum term?

options.A: State is recomputed from scratch each iteration
options.B: In a global cache indexed by layer id
options.C: By giving the weights and bias of every layer their own copy of the optimizer

questions.explanation: This is why append_layer deep-copies the optimizer; it also means each parameter set could in principle use a different optimizer.

questions.question: What does the Flatten layer do?

options.A: It sorts the activations by magnitude
options.B: It averages over the spatial dimensions
options.D: It reshapes a multi-dimensional input into a one-dimensional feature vector per sample

questions.explanation: Flatten is the adapter that lets a convolutional or pooling layer feed a fully connected layer.

questions.question: What does Flatten.backward(error_tensor) do?

options.A: It returns zeros because Flatten has no parameters
options.B: It reshapes the error tensor back to the shape of the forward input
options.C: It averages the error over the flattened dimension
options.D: It returns the error tensor unchanged

questions.explanation: Flatten is a pure reshape, so the backward pass simply reverses it; the stored input shape is needed for that.

questions.question: What three arguments does the Conv constructor receive?
questions.explanation: convolution_shape encodes the channels and the spatial extent; num_kernels sets the number of output channels.

questions.question: How does convolution_shape distinguish a 1-D from a 2-D convolutional layer?

options.D: By the length of stride_shape only

questions.explanation: c is the number of input channels; m and n are the spatial extents of the kernel.

questions.question: What is the expected input layout of the Conv layer for the 2-D case?
questions.explanation: Batch first, then channels, then the spatial dimensions; the 1-D case drops x and uses b, c, y.

questions.question: Which padding does the exercise require for the convolutional layer?

options.B: Valid padding, so the output shrinks
options.C: No padding is applied in either direction
options.D: Zero padding ('same'), so input and output share the spatial shape at stride 1

questions.explanation: Same padding keeps the spatial dimensions when the stride is 1, which simplifies stacking layers.

questions.question: The exercise slides state that a convolutional layer applies different padding along different axes. Which combination is correct?

options.A: 'same' across the image plane and 'valid' across the channel axis
options.B: 'valid' across the image plane and 'same' across the channel axis

questions.explanation: The kernel spans all input channels at once (fully connected across channels, i.e. valid), while the spatial axes are zero-padded.

questions.question: Which properties must the Conv layer expose for the gradient tests?
questions.explanation: The parameters live in members called weights and bias, and the tests read the two gradient properties after backward().

questions.question: Why may the Conv layer need two copies of the optimizer object?

options.A: To run forward and backward in parallel
options.B: Because the bias is handled separately from the other weights, and each parameter set needs its own optimizer state
options.C: Because 1-D and 2-D convolutions use different optimizers
options.D: To support both training and testing phases

questions.explanation: Stateful optimizers such as Adam would otherwise mix the moment estimates of the weights and the bias.

questions.question: The exercise hints at using correlation in the forward pass and convolution in the backward pass. Why?

options.A: The backward pass computes a different mathematical operation entirely
options.B: It avoids explicitly flipping the kernels, since convolution and correlation differ exactly by that flip
options.C: Convolution cannot be applied to multi-channel data
options.D: Correlation is faster than convolution on all hardware

questions.explanation: Because the two operations differ only by a spatial flip of the kernel, choosing them appropriately keeps the implementation flip-free.

questions.question: The exercise permits implementing striding wastefully. What does that mean?

options.A: Padding the input with extra zeros before convolving
options.B: Computing the convolution twice and averaging
options.C: Performing a stride-1 convolution and then subsampling the result
options.D: Convolving each channel separately in a Python loop

questions.explanation: It costs unnecessary computation but is much simpler; the backward pass must then re-insert zero rows and columns where samples were dropped.

questions.question: Which two arguments does the Pooling constructor receive?
questions.explanation: The ordering matches the convolutional layer's convention.

questions.question: Which padding does the Pooling layer use, and what follows from that?

options.A: The same padding as the preceding Conv layer
options.B: Valid padding, so border elements may be discarded
options.C: Same padding, so the spatial size is preserved
options.D: Reflection padding, so no information is lost

questions.explanation: Unlike the convolutional layer, pooling applies no zero padding, which must be accounted for when sizing the output tensor.

questions.question: For which case must the Pooling layer be implemented in exercise 2?
questions.explanation: In contrast to Conv, which must handle 1-D and 2-D, the pooling layer is required only for 2-D inputs.

questions.question: What must the max-pooling forward pass store for its backward pass?

options.A: The mean of each pooling window
options.B: Nothing; the backward pass is a plain reshape
options.C: The locations of the maxima, so the error can be routed back to them

questions.explanation: Max pooling routes the incoming error only to the argmax positions; every other element receives zero.

questions.question: Why do pooling layers reduce overfitting according to the exercise description?

options.C: They add noise to the activations
options.D: They reduce dimensionality and introduce a degree of scale and translation invariance

questions.explanation: Fewer dimensions mean fewer downstream parameters, and small shifts of the input leave the pooled maximum unchanged.

questions.question: Exercise 3 adds a boolean member to BaseLayer. Which one, and why?

options.A: memorize, to carry hidden state between sequences
options.B: trainable, to mark layers with parameters
options.D: testing_phase, because some layers behave differently during training and testing

questions.explanation: Dropout and batch normalization need to know the phase; NeuralNetwork sets it through its phase property in train() and test().

questions.question: What is the purpose of the new Optimizer base class introduced in exercise 3?

options.A: To hold the learning rate schedule
options.B: To provide add_regularizer(regularizer) and store the regularizer for every optimizer
options.C: To implement calculate_update once for all optimizers
options.D: To replace the deep copy in append_layer

questions.explanation: All optimizers inherit from it so that any of them can apply an L1 or L2 constraint.

questions.question: Which two methods must L1_Regularizer and L2_Regularizer provide?
questions.explanation: calculate_gradient supplies the (sub-)gradient for the optimizer; norm supplies the value added to the data loss.

questions.question: What does the constructor argument alpha of the regularizer classes represent?
questions.explanation: alpha scales how strongly the norm penalty influences the total loss and the gradient.

questions.question: Which prior assumption does L1 regularization encode, according to the exercise description?
questions.explanation: L1 pushes many weights exactly to zero, while L2 shrinks all weights towards small values.

questions.question: Why does the L1 regularizer supply a sub-gradient rather than a gradient?

options.A: Because L1 is applied only every second iteration
options.B: Because the L1 norm is not differentiable at zero
options.D: Because the gradient would be too large numerically

questions.explanation: The absolute value has a kink at the origin, so a sub-gradient (e.g. the sign function) is used there.

questions.question: Which layers must contribute their norm(weights) to the regularization loss in NeuralNetwork?

options.A: All layers, including ReLU and Flatten
options.C: Only the last layer before the loss

questions.explanation: Every layer that carries trainable weights sums its norm into the total; parameter-free layers contribute nothing.

questions.question: What does the constructor argument 'probability' of the Dropout layer mean in exercise 3?

options.A: The fraction of units to keep
options.B: The probability that dropout is active at all
options.C: The fraction of units to drop
options.D: The variance of the dropout noise

questions.explanation: The description defines it as the fraction of units to keep, so a value of 0.25 keeps a quarter of the activations.

questions.question: In inverted dropout, what happens during training?

options.A: The loss is divided by p
options.B: The weights are scaled by 1/p after each update
options.C: Surviving activations are scaled by 1/p during training, so nothing has to change at test time
options.D: Activations are scaled by p at test time

questions.explanation: Rescaling during training keeps the expected activation constant, which is exactly what lets the test-time forward pass be the identity.

questions.question: How must Dropout.forward behave in the testing phase?

options.A: It passes the input through unchanged
options.B: It scales the input by p
options.C: It drops units with the same probability as during training

questions.explanation: With inverted dropout the scaling has already been applied during training, so test-time forward is a pass-through.

questions.question: Which regularization problem does dropout specifically address, according to the exercise?

options.D: Co-adaptation of units, by enforcing more independent weights

questions.explanation: Because a unit cannot rely on any particular partner being present, the layer learns more redundant, independent features.

questions.question: What argument does the BatchNormalization constructor receive?

options.A: channels, the number of channels of the input tensor
options.B: The epsilon used for numerical stability

questions.explanation: The same argument covers both the vector case and the image case, where a scalar mean and variance are kept per channel.

questions.question: How does BatchNormalization.initialize set the weights gamma and the bias beta?

options.C: gamma with zeros and beta with ones
options.D: gamma with ones and beta with zeros, ignoring any assigned initializer

questions.explanation: Starting at gamma=1, beta=0 makes the layer an identity transform of the normalised signal, so it has no impact at the beginning of training.

questions.question: Which epsilon does the exercise require inside batch normalization?
questions.explanation: The tests compare against a reference implementation, so a very small epsilon is required to stay within tolerance.

questions.question: How does BatchNormalization estimate the mean and variance used at test time?

options.A: By reusing the current test batch statistics
options.C: By a moving average updated during training, initialised with the batch statistics of the first batch
options.D: By recomputing them over the whole training set after training

questions.explanation: Computing the true training set statistics is expensive, so an online estimate of the form mu = alpha*mu + (1-alpha)*mu_batch is kept.

questions.question: In the moving average formulas of batch normalization, what does the exponent (k) denote?

options.B: An exponent, as in Adam's bias correction

questions.explanation: The exercise slides flag this explicitly, because in Adam the same-looking superscript is an exponent rather than an index.

questions.question: In convolutional batch normalization, over what are the statistics computed?

options.A: One mean and variance per pixel position
options.B: One scalar mean and variance per channel, shared across the spatial positions and the batch
options.C: A single scalar for the entire tensor
options.D: One mean and variance per sample in the batch

questions.explanation: This mirrors how a convolution shares one kernel across all spatial positions.

questions.question: What does the reformat(tensor) method of BatchNormalization do?

options.A: It casts the tensor to float64
options.B: It normalises the tensor to zero mean and unit variance
options.C: It transposes the batch and channel axes only
options.D: It converts between the 4-D image-like and the 2-D vector-like tensor shape, in both directions

questions.explanation: One method handles both directions by branching on the shape, which lets the vector implementation be reused for images.

questions.question: Helpers.py provides compute_bn_gradients(...). What does it compute?

options.A: The full backward pass of the layer
options.B: The gradient with respect to gamma and beta
options.C: The gradient with respect to the inputs, but not with respect to the weights
options.D: The moving average of mean and variance

questions.explanation: The description notes explicitly that the weight gradients still have to be derived and implemented yourself.

questions.question: Which optimizer settings does the optional LeNet task of exercise 3 prescribe?

options.A: Adam with learning rate 5e-4 and an L2 regularizer of weight 4e-4
options.B: Adam with learning rate 1e-3 and an L1 regularizer of weight 1e-4
options.D: SGD with learning rate 0.01 and no regularizer

questions.explanation: The task also asks you to store the optimizer and initializer settings together with the network.

questions.question: Why must the data_layer be excluded when a network is saved with pickle in exercise 3?

options.A: It contains the labels, which would leak the test set
options.B: It is too large to store
options.D: It is a generator object, which pickle cannot process

questions.explanation: __getstate__ drops it and __setstate__ restores the dropped members as None, so the data layer must be set again after loading.

questions.question: Which activation function and which output layer does the LeNet variant of exercise 3 use?

options.A: ReLU activations and a linear output
options.B: TanH activations and a Sigmoid output
options.C: Sigmoid activations and a SoftMax output
options.D: ReLU activations and a SoftMax output

questions.explanation: The exercise deliberately modernises the classic architecture and also ignores its original lack of padding.

questions.question: For TanH and Sigmoid, the exercise asks you to store the activations rather than the input tensor. Why?

options.A: Because the input is not available in the backward pass
options.B: Because the derivative of both functions can be expressed purely in terms of the activation

questions.explanation: For the sigmoid the derivative is a(1-a) and for tanh it is 1-a^2, so caching the output is sufficient for the dynamic programming step.

questions.question: What three arguments does the RNN constructor of exercise 3 receive?
questions.explanation: The hidden state is initialised with zeros and has dimension hidden_size.

questions.question: What does the memorize property of the RNN layer control?

options.A: Whether the weights are shared across time steps
options.B: Whether the layer caches its gradients
options.C: Whether the hidden state is carried over to the next forward call or reset to zeros
options.D: Whether the layer stores its inputs for the backward pass

questions.explanation: Carrying the state over is what turns backpropagation through time into truncated backpropagation through time.

questions.question: Which dimension of the input tensor does the RNN layer treat as the time axis?
questions.explanation: The exercise reuses the existing tensor layout and reinterprets the batch dimension as the sequence over which the recurrence runs.

questions.question: In the RNN layer, which weights does the property gradient_weights refer to?

options.A: The sum of all weights in the layer
options.B: The weights involved in computing the hidden state, as a single stacked tensor
options.C: The weights of the output gate only
options.D: The weights of the embedded TanH layer

questions.explanation: If the hidden state is produced by one fully connected layer fed with the stacked hidden state and input, those are the weights of the whole class.

questions.question: Why is it wrong to update the hidden-gate weights at every time step while iterating backwards through TBPTT?

options.A: Because the optimizer would run out of memory
options.B: Because the gradients would become exactly zero
options.C: Because the hidden state would be reset
options.D: Because the Elman cell shares one weight set across all time steps, so changing them mid-pass makes forward and backward inconsistent

questions.explanation: You would be changing the function while differentiating it; the remedy is to accumulate the gradients and update once the backward pass is finished.

questions.question: Why does exercise 3 introduce the LSTM as an alternative to the Elman cell?

options.A: Because the LSTM needs fewer parameters
options.B: Because Elman cells cannot process sequences
options.C: Because Elman networks suffer severely from the vanishing gradient problem
options.D: Because the LSTM avoids the need for backpropagation through time

questions.explanation: The gated cell state gives gradients a path through time that is not repeatedly squashed by a saturating non-linearity.

questions.question: Under what condition do the LSTM unit tests run in exercise 3?

options.A: Only when a file LSTM.py exists in the Layers folder
options.B: Always, they are part of the default suite
options.C: Only after the RNN tests pass

questions.explanation: The optional task is detected by the presence of the file, so the suite does not fail for students who skip it.

questions.question: Which extra capabilities must the RNN layer provide so regularizers can be reused?

options.A: An optimizer property and a calculate_regularization_loss() method
options.D: A dropout property and a norm() method

questions.explanation: Together with initialize(weights_initializer, bias_initializer), this makes the RNN behave like any other trainable layer.

questions.question: What is the classification task of the exercise 4 PyTorch challenge?

options.B: Predicting stock prices from time series
options.D: Detecting cracks and inactive regions in electroluminescence images of solar cells

questions.explanation: A current is applied to the module so the silicon emits near-infrared light, which specialised cameras capture for visual defect inspection.

questions.question: Why is a SoftMax output with cross entropy unsuitable for the exercise 4 task?

options.A: Because there are only two classes
options.B: Because the labels 'crack' and 'inactive' are not mutually exclusive, so it is a multi-label problem
options.D: Because SoftMax cannot be used with convolutional networks

questions.explanation: SoftMax assumes exactly one class is active; here a cell can be both cracked and inactive, which calls for per-class sigmoids and BCE.

questions.question: Which loss is used in export_onnx.py of exercise 4, and what does that imply about the output layer?

options.A: BCELoss, so the model already applies a sigmoid to its outputs
options.B: CrossEntropyLoss, so the model outputs raw logits
options.C: MSELoss, so the task is treated as regression
options.D: NLLLoss, so the model outputs log-probabilities

questions.explanation: t.nn.BCELoss expects probabilities, which matches the Sigmoid at the end of the prescribed ResNet.

questions.question: What must ChallengeDataset inherit from, and what does its constructor receive?

options.A: torch.nn.Module; it receives the model and the optimizer
options.B: torch.utils.data.DataLoader; it receives a batch size
options.C: torch.utils.data.Dataset; it receives a pandas dataframe and a mode flag ('train' or 'val')
options.D: torchvision.datasets.ImageFolder; it receives a directory path

questions.explanation: Subclassing Dataset means implementing __len__ and __getitem__ so a DataLoader can batch and shuffle the samples.

questions.question: Which two methods must be overwritten in ChallengeDataset?
questions.explanation: __len__ reports the number of samples and __getitem__ returns one (image, label) tuple as torch tensors.

questions.question: Why does __getitem__ call skimage.color.gray2rgb on the solar cell images?

options.A: Because PyTorch cannot store single-channel tensors
options.B: To improve the contrast of the defects
options.D: Because the raw data is grayscale but the network expects three input channels

questions.explanation: The prescribed ResNet starts with Conv2D(3, 64, 7, 2), and the normalisation constants are also given per three channels.

questions.question: Which transforms must the Compose object of exercise 4 contain at minimum?
questions.explanation: Normalize needs the mean and standard deviation of the training data, both of which the skeleton provides.

questions.question: Why does the exercise suggest defining two different transform chains?

options.A: Because validation images have a different resolution
options.B: Because the labels differ between the two modes
options.C: Because augmentation belongs in the training chain but not in the validation chain
options.D: Because the GPU requires a different data type at validation time

questions.explanation: Augmenting the validation set would make the reported score depend on random transforms rather than on model quality.

questions.question: What does one ResBlock in the exercise 4 ResNet consist of?

options.A: The sequence (Conv2D, BatchNorm, ReLU) repeated twice, with the skip connection added after the second BatchNorm and ReLU applied afterwards
options.B: A single Conv2D followed by ReLU
options.C: Two fully connected layers with a skip connection
options.D: A Conv2D followed by MaxPool and Dropout

questions.explanation: The ordering matters: the shortcut is added before the final activation, not after it.

questions.question: What filter size do all Conv2D layers inside a ResBlock use?
questions.explanation: Only the stem convolution of the network uses a 7x7 kernel; inside the blocks the kernels are 3x3.

questions.question: Within a ResBlock, which convolution carries the stride?

options.C: The first convolution; the second uses no stride
options.D: Neither; striding happens in the pooling layer

questions.explanation: The first convolution performs the spatial downsampling and the second keeps the resolution, so the block halves the size at most once.

questions.question: Why must the input of a ResBlock sometimes be transformed before it is added to the output?

options.A: Because the input has to be normalised first
options.B: Because the stride and the number of channels can change inside the block, so the shapes would not match
options.C: Because the addition would otherwise be a concatenation
options.D: Because ReLU cannot be applied to the raw input

questions.explanation: The recommended fix is a 1x1 convolution with the matching stride and channel count, followed by a batch norm.

questions.question: In the prescribed ResNet, what are the channel counts of the four ResBlocks in order?
questions.explanation: Only the first block keeps its channel count; the remaining three double the channels while halving the resolution with stride 2.

questions.question: Which layers follow the last ResBlock in the exercise 4 architecture?
questions.explanation: Global average pooling reduces each of the 512 feature maps to one value, so the classifier sees a 512-dimensional vector regardless of input size.

questions.question: The final fully connected layer is FC(512, 2). What do the two outputs represent?

options.A: The two mutually exclusive classes 'defect' and 'no defect'
options.B: The mean and variance of the prediction
options.D: The bounding box coordinates of the defect

questions.explanation: Each output gets its own sigmoid, since a cell can exhibit both defects, only one, or neither.

questions.question: What is the early stopping criterion used in the exercise 4 Trainer?

options.A: Stop when the training loss stops decreasing
options.B: Stop when the learning rate reaches zero
options.C: Stop after a fixed number of epochs
options.D: Stop when the validation loss does not decrease for a specified number of epochs

questions.explanation: The patience is passed to the Trainer as early_stopping_patience; a value of -1 disables it, in which case an epoch count must be given.

questions.question: Why must the gradients be reset at the start of each training step in PyTorch?

options.A: Because the model would stay in evaluation mode
options.B: Because the optimizer would otherwise use the wrong learning rate
options.C: Because the loss would otherwise be negative
options.D: Because PyTorch accumulates (sums up) gradients on every backward() call by default

questions.explanation: Without zeroing, each step would apply the sum of all gradients seen so far rather than the current batch's gradient.

questions.question: Why does the validation step call model.eval() before iterating the validation set?

options.A: To move the model to the CPU
options.D: Because layers such as Dropout and BatchNorm behave differently during training and testing

questions.explanation: This is the PyTorch equivalent of the testing_phase flag added to BaseLayer in exercise 3.

questions.question: Why are gradients disabled during validation in the exercise 4 Trainer?

options.A: Because the validation loss would otherwise be wrong
options.B: Because the validation set has no labels
options.C: Because no weight update happens, so building the graph only wastes memory and time

questions.explanation: Wrapping the loop in a no-grad context skips the autograd bookkeeping entirely.

questions.question: Which metric does the exercise 4 skeleton import from scikit-learn for evaluation?
questions.explanation: The F1 score balances precision and recall, which matters because defective cells are the minority in this dataset.

questions.question: What is the purpose of save_onnx in the exercise 4 Trainer?

options.A: To export the trained model in a portable format for submission to the leader board
options.D: To save the training checkpoints during the run

questions.explanation: ONNX is a framework-independent exchange format; the export runs the model once on a dummy input to trace the graph.

questions.question: The ONNX export declares dynamic_axes for input and output with batch_size at axis 0. Why?

options.A: So the number of classes can change
options.B: So the exported model accepts an arbitrary batch size at inference time
options.C: So the model can accept images of arbitrary spatial size
options.D: So the model can run on multiple GPUs

questions.explanation: Everything else is traced with fixed shapes; marking axis 0 dynamic keeps the batch dimension free.

questions.question: How does the exercise 4 pipeline obtain a validation set?

options.A: A separate CSV file with validation samples is provided
options.B: By splitting the data from data.csv with a train-test split
options.C: By holding out the last batch of every epoch
options.D: By using the leader board test set

questions.explanation: train.py imports train_test_split from sklearn.model_selection for exactly this purpose.

questions.question: What does each row of data.csv in exercise 4 contain?

options.A: The raw pixel values of one image
options.B: The image path and a bounding box
options.C: The image path plus two numbers indicating 'crack' and 'inactive'
options.D: The image path and a single class index

questions.explanation: Two independent binary flags per row is what makes this a multi-label rather than a multi-class problem.

questions.question: Why does the exercise recommend training on university machines rather than a laptop?

options.A: Because PyTorch is licensed per machine
options.B: Because training neural networks needs substantial compute, and those machines provide GPUs
options.C: Because the unit tests only run there
options.D: Because the dataset may not be copied off campus

questions.explanation: The recommendation is to debug locally and run the actual training remotely, loading the python3 and torch modules first.

questions.question: Which two defect types does the exercise 4 dataset distinguish, and how do they relate?

options.A: Cracks and dirt; they never co-occur
options.B: Cracks and cell breakage; the second implies the first
options.C: Cracks and inactive regions; inactive regions are often caused by cracks but are labelled independently
options.D: Inactive regions and shading; both are labelled as one class

questions.explanation: A cell is only labelled cracked when the crack is visible, and the two labels are treated as independent despite the physical link.

questions.question: Which of the following layers in the exercise framework are trainable? (Select all that apply)
questions.explanation: ReLU is a fixed non-linearity with no parameters; the other three all carry weights that an optimizer updates.

questions.question: Which layers of the exercise framework behave differently in the training and the testing phase? (Select all that apply)
questions.explanation: This is exactly why exercise 3 adds the testing_phase member to BaseLayer and the phase property to NeuralNetwork.

questions.question: Which optimizers are implemented across exercises 1 and 2? (Select all that apply)
